column1: Text

At the dawn of the 1900s, the Model T Ford, along with other similar vehicles, changed how people moved. Now, a big question pops up: do we depend on cars too much? I think cutting back on car use is a must.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's story, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights why this is so important. Cars are everywhere, from Shanghai to Chicago. Because of this, experts say it's hard to reduce pollution from cars. Get this: cars make up 12% of Europe's pollution. But in the U.S., it jumps to 50% because we use cars so much! Also, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," really shows how bad cars are for the environment. Paris stopped people from driving to fight smog.

On the flip side, Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," gives us hope. Millions of Colombians joined a day without cars. They walked, biked, or took buses, and the streets were super quiet. Every year, this event bans personal cars but lets buses and taxis run, which makes the air way better. Plus, Bogota has changed a lot, adding parks, fun places, sidewalks, and stores.

Basically, using cars less and having car-free events is great. It makes the air cleaner and cities nicer. Bogota's car-free day shows that we can cut down traffic and make city life better. If we did the same in America, like encouraging biking and walking for short trips, we could have a better future. In the end, pushing for less car use is a smart and good idea.

Every country needs good transportation, so cars and trains are super important for getting around. However, so many cars cause major issues because pollution goes up everywhere! All that driving makes the air really nasty. It's crucial that we quickly find ways to cut pollution down.

Consider Vauban, this German town that's pretty cool. Not a ton of folks use cars there. Most stores and important places are super close by, so getting around is simple. You'll still see cars, but many folks in Vauban don't even own one. Get this: more than half the families sold their cars when they moved there. They claim to be less stressed because driving less is nice. Cars cause 12% of all pollution these days, but, like, in busy US cities, it might reach 50%.

So, Paris was super smoggy, and they tried something new. On Mondays, no even-numbered license plates were allowed. If you broke the rule, bam, huge fines! The next day, odd numbers got the same deal. In five days, traffic went down 60%. The diesel cars that France loves made smog much worse. Even so, the ban didn't last since they stopped doing the every-other-day thing.

Picture a car-free day like Bogota, Colombia. Once a year, only taxis and buses are okay. Pollution goes way down on this day, and they've done it since the 90s. Later on, they put in bike paths, parks, and places to chill. It might be tough, but ditching cars helps the planet.

Okay, we probably won’t fully dump cars soon, but countries should try to pollute less and make driving less appealing. Taking steps to beat air pollution will improve life. Sure, we use cars a lot, but too much driving is bad for the world and us.

Is America ditching cars? Elisabeth Rosenthal asks a good question. Basically, driving less is getting popular, and that's awesome! Imagine: way fewer accidents, healthier people, and cleaner air because we're driving less. Do we even need cars like we used to?

First off, driving less is great for our health and safety – super important, right? Andrew Selsky said the streets in Colombia felt so calm on a car-free day. Fewer cars mean less stress! Traffic jams mess up our day and make us angry. So, less driving, less traffic, less stress! Heidrun Walter felt happier and less stressed once she sold her car. And, we get more exercise too. Cars are convenient, sure, but we forget to move. Selsky saw tons of new parks and places to play sports. If there were fewer cars, we could have more parks, which would make us want to get moving. Basically, driving less means less stress and a healthier life!

Also, driving less helps the planet. Some people might not care, but it's really important. Pollution is bad for everything, and cars cause a lot of it. Rosenthal says that driving less "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving, less pollution – a better chance for the future. Pollution is a huge problem, and drivers make it worse.

Plus, we might not need cars as much as we think. Lots of people think they HAVE to have them, but are they hooked? Technology has changed us, and we're forgetting about nature. Being too dependent on anything isn't good. David Goldberg says "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some people might not agree, but relying on something bad isn't progress. Rosenthal points out that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can even afford a car. People who rely too much on cars have forgotten about nature and don’t always think about the environment. Driving less could help with that.

So, in short, less driving could mean we're healthier, there's less pollution, and we don't need cars as much. It's a cleaner, safer world. Maybe America's getting better by driving less and thinking about what's good for everyone.

Ever find yourself just constantly driving around? Whether commuting to your job or simply heading to the grocery store? It's astounding the frequency with which everyone utilizes their vehicles, even for short errands. It's easy to overlook the environmental impact of all this driving. Perhaps consider skipping the car today? People often protest, "But I absolutely require my vehicle!" However, reducing driving is achievable. Cities such as Havana, Paris, and Bogotá demonstrate its feasibility. Indeed, decreased driving benefits both you and the environment.

Surprisingly, reduced driving could potentially boost your happiness! Heidrun Walter suggested that individuals experience considerably less stress when they abstain from driving. Reflect on the frustration you encounter in congested traffic. Navigating routes, dealing with aggressive drivers—it all contributes to heightened stress levels. Major metropolises such as New York and Paris are notorious for their appalling traffic and polluted atmosphere. In his piece titled "Paris bans driving due to smog," Robert Duffer elucidated how warm air traps vehicle emissions, which is extremely detrimental. Duffer also detailed Paris's temporary ban on automobiles due to severe pollution, which resulted in rapid air quality improvements! Consequently, individuals could opt for walking, cycling, or even rollerblading. These alternatives are healthier and promote greater happiness compared to being stuck in gridlock.

Should we diminish our reliance on cars, urban environments would undergo significant improvements. Pollution inflicts harm on vegetation and generates unpleasant odors, fostering an overall sense of gloom. Decreased driving leads to fewer harmful emissions and cleaner urban landscapes. In "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars," Elisabeth Rosenthal noted that fewer vehicles facilitate increased pedestrian activity, which fosters more sidewalks and enticing shops. Andrew Selsky highlighted the emergence of new parks, recreational areas, well-maintained sidewalks, eateries, and stores. The EPA favors areas with reduced car presence, as Rosenthal explained, due to its substantial reduction in pollution. Bogotá, Colombia, even enforces a "no car" day and imposes fines on drivers who violate the regulations. While fines are irritating, they effectively reduce smog! Essentially: less driving is excellent for you, enhances cities, and aids in purifying the environment.

Okay, so cars, right? People dig 'em, no doubt, but they're REALLY important. I mean, we NEED them to get around! Sure, there's public transportation, but a lot of us would rather ride solo. But, too many cars cause pollution and stress. Swapping to buses or trains means you pay less on repairs, there's less traffic, and the air gets cleaner. So, driving less is a smart move.

Using cars less is good for us and the planet, mostly 'cause the air gets better. Car engines spit out nasty stuff. One car? No big deal. But TONS of cars, like in Paris, are a serious problem. So, Paris actually BANNED cars 'cause of the bad air! Robert Duffer pointed out that cars with even-numbered plates couldn't drive for a while and got fined if they did, then it was the odd numbers' turn. That cut traffic by 60%! And Andrew Selsky said that Bogota, Colombia, has days with no cars to fight pollution, get people to try other ways to get around, and help the environment. All over the world, driving less is seen as a win!

Plus, think about chilling out! Worrying about traffic, fixing your car, and all that noise makes you stressed. Less driving, less stress. No traffic jams, repair bills, or loud noises makes life easier. Elisabeth Rosenthal said Hartmut Walter told her, "I was always tense driving. Now I feel better.” That shows how not driving can ease your mind. Also, Andrew Selsky says car-free days "are a great way to unwind..." Taking care of your head is key because less stress makes life better.

Bottom line? Driving less is a win-win for us and the world. The planet needs a break, and stress sucks. Driving less helps both. Yeah, it can be hard to change, but places are doing it and it works! It makes for a healthier, happier world.

Isn't the Electoral College strange? We don't vote straight for the president. Instead, we select folks to do it for us, left or right. But, uh oh! Source two says electors can ditch our votes! Is that right? We vote hoping for good stuff, but this system is messy. The worst part? It's winner-takes-all!

We don't directly elect the president. Source two says we choose electors. States get some. Then, these electors vote. There are 538 electors. You need 270 to win. State elector count depends on its Congress members. Truly, every vote should count! Dump the Electoral College, and every vote matters. Candidates must earn support, and ties? Rare. Now, if most in your state want someone else, your vote means nothing. Ditch the college; your vote counts!

Source two brings up "faithless" electors. Some might switch and vote for the other side. What a betrayal! A Republican voting Democrat cancels all Republican votes. We elect every four years. Electors changing minds? No way!

Sources two and three point out "winner-take-all." It's rough. If you lose, votes are gone. The winner gets it all! Loser voters may as well have wanted the winner.

Senators, lose the Electoral College! It's flawed. Some votes matter more. Electors can do what they want. And "winner-take-all"? It trashes votes for losers.

Esteemed Senator,

I am writing to you today regarding a crucial reform: our presidential election process. Considering the abolishment of the Electoral College is essential. Immediately, we must establish a more equitable system representing every citizen! Currently, the archaic system is unjust; electors can defy the popular vote - unacceptable!

I concede altering the Electoral College poses a challenge. However, the long-term benefits outweigh the difficulties. A direct popular vote system will compel candidates to actively seek support from every region. Furthermore, we must address the issue of potential ties and reinforce fairness. Thus, while abolishing the Electoral College entirely may not be viable, rectifying its flaws is imperative.

Currently, electors wield excessive power in determining the outcome. The "winner-takes-all" allocation of votes within the Electoral College breeds disenfranchisement and a sense of insignificance among voters. Candidates disproportionately concentrate their resources in a handful of "swing" states, neglecting the rest. Consider the 2000 election, in which 17 states were virtually ignored. Due to these issues, the Electoral College is outdated and requires either reform or elimination.

Candidates strategically select electors to bolster their chances of victory. These electors garner support without requiring candidates to campaign extensively in every state. Essentially, the candidate awaits the outcome. A candidate can win with minimal effort, as electors possess the ultimate decision-making power. This creates potential for corrupt practices such as bribery within crucial states - a deeply concerning prospect.

When citizens cast their ballots, they are not directly electing candidates. They are, in essence, selecting electors, an anomaly. Electors are chosen via various methods, including state conventions or by the candidates themselves. A significant problem with the Electoral College is its susceptibility to unforeseen events. For instance, in 1960, some Louisiana residents entertained the idea of replacing Democratic electors with individuals opposed to John F. Kennedy.

Fundamentally, the Electoral College is an antique artifact from a bygone era. It grants electors undue power, undermining the democratic rights of voters. While it may have served a purpose in the past, it is incompatible with the principles of modern democracy.

Alright, pay attention! We are encircled by automobiles, correct? Their operation appears straightforward. However, consider a reduction in our driving habits. Reflect upon this: purer atmospheric conditions and amplified personal savings. Honestly, decreased driving? It's an excellent proposition.

Consider Paris, for instance. According to Robert Duffer, "Paris bans driving due to smog." Their approach demonstrated considerable ingenuity. On one day, vehicles bearing odd-numbered license plates were authorized to operate; on the subsequent day, solely those with even numbers. Infringement of regulations resulted in monetary penalties! Furthermore, they rendered public transit gratuitous for a week! Congestion and smog? Vanished! The quality of the air improved, and citizens realized economic benefits. Duffer noted a substantial decline in traffic volume – approximately 60%! He stated, "Traffic jams dropped 60 percent in Paris, after five days of smog so thick… it was almost as bad as Beijing, China, a city known for pollution." Despite acute smog conditions, a reduction in vehicular traffic yielded a significant positive impact.

Now, turn your attention to Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal reported, "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They implemented a radical strategy, entirely eliminating automobiles. Approximately 70% do not possess personal vehicles due to exorbitant parking fees outside the town limits, and the general prohibition of automobiles within the area. Residents express contentment. Heidrun Walter remarked, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." A considerable proportion of young adults refrain from obtaining driver's licenses! Rosenthal elucidated in "The end of car culture" that automobiles merely facilitate transportation. There is minimal emphasis on the vehicle itself, with primary concern directed towards convenient mobility.

The core idea centers on the utility of automobiles, as well as the availability of buses, trains, bicycles, and carpools. And what's more? Certain alternatives are provided without charge! Furthermore, a decrease in pollution and congestion constitutes a substantial enhancement for both personal and planetary well-being.

Plumer straight up disses the Electoral College. Source 2 calls it ancient and dumb. Tons of people agree, saying it's a flat-out disaster or just plain stinky. Some argue it prevents ties or boosts big states, but most aren't buying what they're selling. They're screaming for a simple popular vote because it's better, period.

The real kicker is that votes are totally unequal. Electors wield way too much power, unlike average Joes. If we voted directly, every single vote would carry the same weight. Also, electors are open to bribes or getting swayed, which is awful for democracy.

Tons of folks think the Electoral College is a relic. Plumer (Source 2) even quotes Bob Dole yelling, "Ditch the Electoral College!" Many feel the same way, wanting a direct vote and skipping the Electoral College altogether. Some joke a kid with darts could pick a better president, seriously. Plumer (Source 2) reveals, "Over 60% of voters want a direct election," and even more do since 2000.

Seriously, the Electoral College is just plain strange. It's this twisty system where electors, not us, pick the top dog. Plumer (Source 2) explains, "You vote for electors, then they vote for the president. Who are these electors? Just normal folks. Who picks them? Depends on the state. Can we control them? Not always! Do people mess up? Sometimes!" Makes you wonder who's really calling the shots.

Yeah, the Electoral College might have a couple of perks, like keeping things from going haywire, but most think it's not worth the headache. A simple vote by the people seems way more fair. As Plumer (Source 2) puts it, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

Right from the start in the United States, the Electoral College was placed in the Constitution. Keeping it is a good idea because, up to this point, it's been effective. Why try to improve something that functions properly? Staying with the Electoral College is a wise choice; here's a quick explanation in five points.

Alright, the Electoral College does have some downsides. For instance, electors *could* disobey and vote according to their own preferences. Really, we're casting ballots for electors rather than the president, and they're responsible for making the final decision. They have the potential to disregard the public's will and act independently. There is also the possibility of a state submitting two different sets of electors, resulting in chaos. Additionally, although uncommon, the Electoral College may result in a tie. The "winner-takes-all" structure can cause candidates to skip certain states since one party consistently wins there. Candidates could make a greater effort to gain votes everywhere with a direct vote. However, concerns usually revolve around improbable events; therefore, it is preferable to relax and take into account a different viewpoint.

Because they play a crucial role in determining the winner, voters in swing states are typically more engaged. They stay informed about the news and the candidates because they understand the significance of their votes. Candidates devote significant time and resources to winning them over. Many other states are practically predetermined because one party is extremely well-liked. It's possible that voters in swing states focus more on their preferences and ideals; perhaps they are the ideal people to choose our president.

The Electoral College prevents us from requiring further ballots, which streamlines the procedure. A very tight direct vote could cause significant difficulties without it. When you look at Nixon's victory in '68 and Clinton's in '92, the Electoral College's usefulness is evident; each won with roughly 43% of the vote.

I understand why some voters in states that consistently vote in one direction may disapprove of the Electoral College. Given that their votes may appear inconsequential, they might decide not to vote at all. Individually, a vote carries little weight, and some people chose not to vote in 2012. Some people find that just expressing their preferences is more important than really influencing the election. If you are involved in the government, your vote has a considerably higher chance of having an impact.

Senator,

Listen, ditching the Electoral College is a terrible idea! We've got 538 electors to deal with, you see. Getting over 270 of their votes is how you win the whole shebang. States choose these folks, who usually pledge to a certain candidate. We absolutely *must* keep it around. Why? Because it makes picking a winner super clear, that's why!

So, why stick with it? Well, mostly, the Electoral College hands us a clear win every time, without all the extra fuss. Could it end in a tie? Maybe, but it's pretty rare, even with how the electors are set up now. Think back to 2012, when Obama grabbed nearly 62% of the electoral votes, while only winning 51% of the popular vote. Most states have a winner-takes-all system. Thus, a close win in a state leads to a whole bunch of electoral votes for you. Sure, this makes candidates hyper-focus on swing states, perhaps ignoring others. But usually, the winner gets way more electoral votes than actual votes from the people.

Plus, the President needs to represent *everybody*. So, the Electoral College forces candidates to go after votes everywhere. No single place has enough votes to pick the president alone. Even if you're crazy popular somewhere, you still gotta campaign all over. Grabbing more votes where you're already liked doesn't get you more electoral votes. A president no one supports? That is a recipe for total chaos. People would feel like they don't matter.

In short, keeping the Electoral College is a *must*. It basically stops ties from happening and makes sure the president is working for *all* of us.

Heidrun Walter used to not like vehicles, but now she does! She feels very free. This is all over, with people leaving behind their rides. We used to think vehicles were needed, but now sales are down, and fewer folks are driving. They are finding out that using vehicles less is a good deal. This can surely help stop climate change, stop pollution, and make the economy better for everyone, not just vehicle companies.

Vehicle gases worsen the greenhouse effect, which hurts us and nature. One source says vehicles cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases – almost half in some cities in the US! Less driving means less pollution, which is awesome for our earth. This can help fight big problems like global warming and rising oceans. So, fewer vehicles mean fewer greenhouse gases!

Smog and pollution are bad. They hurt our health and our world. The article says, "After Days of near-record pollution, the city enforced a partial ban on driving to clear the air." The air got much better fast! Doing this more could reduce pollution a lot. It might even get people to try buses, bikes, or walking! One source shows that banning vehicles can lead to new bike paths, parks, sports centers, better sidewalks, and less traffic. Basically, less driving makes city life better!

Lastly, less driving helps the economy in many ways. One source said that in the US, we spend tons of money on roads. If we didn't need vehicles as much, that money could go to schools and other things. Also, less driving saves people money, so they can spend it on other stuff. One source said that shifts in who drives suggest the trend may speed up. There has been a big drop in the percentage of young adults getting a license. This lets us focus on making the economy better. So, less driving means more government money, more savings for people, and growth for all.

In short, using vehicles less is really good. It helps the economy, stops the greenhouse effect, and stops pollution in cities.

Selecting a president? Incredibly important! Their choices? They have major consequences, positive or negative. Now, the Electoral College is meant to represent each state's desires, but is it truly effective? Personally, I believe it does a reasonably good job of showing each state's position and giving us insight into the overall sentiment of the nation.

There's criticism aimed at the Electoral College, with some arguing that it doesn't consistently select the most qualified individual or that it lacks fairness. However, let's not forget that we have the power to CHOOSE! Our opinions are significant, and each individual vote holds immense value – far greater than abstaining from voting altogether. It's not flawless, but what is? All things considered, I think the Electoral College competently tallies the votes.

Devising a superior alternative is challenging. A primary concern is the possibility that the Electoral College may not accurately reflect the popular vote. Yet, I disagree with that notion. Our votes play a role in guiding the Electoral College toward our preferred candidate, and I appreciate how it encourages interstate cooperation by showcasing the prevailing sentiment in each state.

Admittedly, some argue that larger states wield excessive influence within the Electoral College. Perhaps there's some truth to that, but it's somewhat logical, isn't it? States with larger populations should receive a larger share of the votes. Nevertheless, each vote carries equal weight, preventing the dominant states from exerting total control. Even in moments of disagreement, the Electoral College offers a balanced means of understanding the national mood, which is beneficial.

Ultimately, the Electoral College determines each state's preference by utilizing the majority of votes to select a winner. It has limitations, but simply adding up all the votes might be a poorer solution. Every system presents its own set of challenges, but the Electoral College furnishes us with a reasonably structured method for tabulating votes throughout the years. After all, it is impossible to keep everyone happy!

Dear Senator, I've been pondering the best way to choose our President and their cabinet, and I have a suggestion. Basically, we have two options: the Electoral College or counting every single vote from each citizen. After considering both, one stands out as far superior, no question about it.

The Electoral College system involves specific individuals, called electors, casting votes for the President and Vice President. These 538 individuals ultimately determine the winner. I must say, I'm not a fan of this system. We believe we are directly electing the president, but in reality, we are merely selecting electors who then make the crucial decision. Why should such a small group, especially compared to millions of voters, have such immense power in choosing our leader? We're left anxiously waiting to see what they decide. It feels incredibly unfair that their opinions hold so much more weight than those of ordinary people when it comes to governing our nation.

Now, a popular vote system allows each person to directly vote for their preferred presidential candidate and their team. This way, we can clearly express our desired leader, hoping for positive changes. Even if a candidate doesn't win, your vote still matters; it contributes to the overall national voice. The Electoral College might offer a quicker resolution, but the knowledge that your vote truly counts could spark greater involvement and interest in the candidates and their platforms. We gain the power to shape our future, rather than simply hoping the electors are aligned with our beliefs.

Sure, the Electoral College has a few potential advantages, such as ensuring a clear winner. Some might even argue that choosing electors is essentially the same as directly voting. However, these minor benefits pale in comparison to granting every citizen the right to directly vote, a fundamental pillar of democracy, in my humble opinion.

Ultimately, when contemplating the process of selecting the president, the core question becomes: How do you want your voice to resonate? Do you want to be directly involved in the process, or are you comfortable with letting others make the crucial decision for you? For me, the answer is crystal clear: I wholeheartedly support the system that fortifies our democracy and empowers every voice to be heard.

Picture a delightful family stroll through the park, marred, alas, by unpleasant pollution! It's glaringly obvious that our reliance on cars is excessive. Why not consider hopping on the bus occasionally, or perhaps walking, or even carpooling? Seriously, curbing our driving habits yields tangible benefits! Cars come with a hefty price tag, hinder alternative modes of transportation, and inflict harm upon our planet.

Owning a car can drain your wallet more than you realize! Take Vauban, Germany, where a parking spot can set you back a staggering $40,000 – exceeding the cost of some houses! And if you venture to drive on a restricted day in Bogota, Columbia, brace yourself for a $25 fine. Driving in Paris? Be prepared to shell out 22 euros simply because of your license plate! And let's not forget the ever-escalating cost of gas! Affluent individuals may shrug it off, but families with children certainly feel the pinch!

It's all too easy to overlook the merits of public transport. Envision a scenario devoid of buses, trains, or subways. Now, attempt to drive into New York City; I wish you the best of luck! Bogota encourages commuters to embrace buses or walking by imposing fines on drivers who violate car restrictions. Mayor Antanas Mockus was thrilled by the widespread participation, even amidst rainy conditions! Cities such as Cali and Valledupar followed suit, sparking considerable interest. Even New York offers bike-sharing programs. Regardless, public transport is vital for safeguarding our environment.

Good heavens, sometimes the smog hangs so thick that celestial views are obscured. Paris temporarily banned cars in response to excessive pollution levels, resulting in a remarkable 60% improvement in air quality within a mere five days! But, France's preference for diesel fuel, a significant pollutant, complicates matters. Bogota's car-free day offers a welcome respite from smog, while Vauban boasts minimal smog due to limited car usage! Even modest adjustments can yield substantial benefits for our Earth.

Vauban and Bogota serve as testaments to the fact that reduced driving translates to less pollution. Paris bans cars as a measure to combat smog. Vauban's exorbitant parking fees discourage car usage. Bogota fines drivers to promote walking or bus ridership. We must curb our car dependence because it saves money, fosters alternative transportation options, and cleanses the air.

Cutting down on car use benefits our town! Other countries, like France and Colombia, are thinking of new ways for us to travel around. Without always driving, it's a lot more relaxed. Studies even show fewer people around here have cars now. This is a great chance to try out cool ideas, like in Vauban or Bogota, so we don't spend all day stuck in traffic.

Consider this: A German town proves you can live without cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal mentioned that Vauban, Germany, is mostly "car-free." Most people there prefer public transport. The article also said, "70% of families don't own cars, and many sell them when they move there!" Someone shared they feel more peaceful and happy without a car, as they avoid the stress of driving. Lots of places aim to be like this, reducing cars to help the Earth. Look forward to more "smart planning" in the future. If we try driving less, our town could become a car-free paradise.

Bogota, Colombia, hosts car-free days, so people can try out other ways of getting around and clean up the air. People who visit always comment on how much better the air feels. Going "car-free" helps people get more active and makes the city look better. Reports point to more parks and sports fields, big, clean sidewalks, and cool shops and restaurants. It's not just about cleaner air; it makes the city a nicer, more attractive place to be!

Imagine using ideas similar to Bogota and Vauban. Fewer people around here are already buying cars. Some are even skipping driver's ed. One writer noticed that Americans aren't as obsessed with cars these days. Another noted that we drove the most miles a while back, and it's been decreasing since then. Young people play a big role in this change. They use social media, phones, carpools, and buses more, which cuts down on their driving. Car companies might not like it, but many of us think we'll have better and easier ways to travel in the future because of this.

Basically, less driving means less pollution and stress, nicer neighborhoods, more exercise, and saving resources. Cities everywhere are working to reduce cars to help the environment. If we go green and car-free, we'll join a global shift!

Dear Senator, I'm writing to urge you to abolish the Electoral College. My firm conviction is that the presidency should be determined solely by the nationwide popular vote. Frankly, the Electoral College strikes me as deeply unjust and potentially detrimental to the principles enshrined in the U.S. Constitution.

The Electoral College undermines the foundational tenets of our democratic system. While we cast our ballots, the Electoral College can override the collective will, a glaring inequity! This raises questions about the true nature of our democracy. Consider the 2000 election: Gore won the popular vote, yet Bush triumphed due to the Electoral College. As "The Indefensible Electoral College" points out, "Over 60% of voters desire a straightforward election, not this convoluted system." Clearly, people favor a simple method for selecting their leader.

Furthermore, I strongly oppose the Electoral College because it could erode the U.S. Constitution, particularly our fundamental rights. The core principle is that we, the citizens, elect our president. However, when the popular vote winner loses because of the Electoral College, it harms democracy. "The Indefensible Electoral College" raises concerns about potential election issues, asking: "What if a state submits two conflicting slates of electors? This occurred in Hawaii in '60. Fortunately, Nixon conceded to the electors chosen for his rival." Imagine the turmoil if such a scenario reoccurred and an undeserving candidate won! The Electoral College is a recipe for disaster.

In conclusion, let's eliminate the Electoral College! It's inherently unfair and poses a significant threat to our U.S. Constitution. I sincerely hope you will consider this perspective when making decisions on this crucial matter.

The environmental impact of transportation is a common concern, yet the potential environmental benefits of reduced driving are often underestimated. Automobiles are consistent polluters. Before purchasing a vehicle, carefully consider the environmental implications, as the ecological cost may outweigh the convenience.

Consider the multitude of cars, buses, and trucks on the road. Their fuel consumption significantly damages the environment. Combustion byproducts degrade the planet. If current trends continue, ozone depletion could render Earth uninhabitable! Moreover, many individuals overuse vehicles when walking or cycling are viable options. When Paris implemented driving restrictions during periods of poor air quality, traffic decreased by an impressive 60% within five days. Our transportation habits can severely harm the environment.

Vehicle ownership entails substantial hidden costs. In Vauban, Germany, acquiring a car necessitates purchasing a parking space for nearly $40,000, excluding housing expenses! Fuel costs are exorbitant, and insurance premiums further inflate expenses. Additionally, driving can be frightening, stressful, and unpleasant.

Cars, cars, cars! You see them all over. They are such a big thing in how we live. How about no cars? That would be great for the world! Too many cars makes air dirty, and no one wants to breathe that in.

Bogota, Colombia did a "No Car Day". What a neat thing that other cities might want to copy! Instead of driving, people walked, biked, or took a bus. And guess what? The city was way cleaner! So what if it rained a bit? Most people still did it. The mayor said the weather wasn't a big deal. They wanted cleaner air by using other ways to get around. But in the U.S.? That's a big ask because we like our cars too much!

Paris tried to fight car smog by banning driving when the air was nasty. People were not happy at first. Reuters said almost 4,000 tickets were given, and 27 cars towed! But traffic went down a lot – like 60%! Even so, the air was still a bit smoggy, like in Beijing. We don't know if folks will keep doing this.

Not driving as much could annoy people because getting around might be harder. BUT, new studies show that fewer Americans are buying cars, using them, or even getting licenses. A professor at Drexel University thinks things are changing. People had to stop driving, so maybe they won't go back. Car use in the U.S. might not go back up, and that's better for the air.

Cars are making the Earth sick everywhere. Not driving is hard, and there are problems with it. But the good things are easy to see! Think of cleaner air and more people walking, biking, and skating. Changing how we use cars? It's really good for the Earth. Less driving? That's like saving the world!

Wanting to drive less? Where you live and how much you *care* matters a lot. Across the globe, cities are testing fewer or even ZERO cars. Places with lots of smog *really* want to get cars off roads so people can breathe easier.

Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb...", shows what car-free life is like. With the right stuff, walking and biking become super appealing. And *that's* important. Thinking about pollution, trying this elsewhere is worth considering.

Some cities are really polluted, it's bad. In Duffer's "Paris bans driving...", Paris's air got so bad they banned cars! People were angry. Breaking the rules meant fines and maybe losing your car! Duffer says "Congestion was down 60 percent..." – showing the ban seriously cut traffic.

Worldwide, plans exist to move people without pollution. Selsky's "Carfree day..." shows how great Bogota did. Millions left cars and walked, biked, or skated – even in rain! Selsky notes Bogota feels *nicer* now. They fixed up "Parks…sidewalks" making the city better.

Things change quickly! Young people ask, "Do I *need* a car?" They prefer cheaper, eco-friendly stuff. The idea of *needing* a car is slowly disappearing, but it's still present.

Ah, the Electoral College! What a controversial beast! Should we keep it or kill it? Is it just…weird? We think we're voting for a president, but *really*, we're picking someone else to pick the president. If America's all about what the people want, shouldn't the person with the most votes just win?

So, what *are* we doing when we vote for president? Are we just choosing someone to vote later? Is that it? This article says we're picking "your candidate's electors." Okay, but if the Constitution says we vote *directly* for president, how does *that* work? Seems kinda backwards, right? And can we *trust* those electors to do what we want? Like the article says, can we *really* control them? Not always! Could your person lose even if most people in the state voted for them? Is the Electoral College stealing our voice, making our votes meaningless? Honestly, it's confusing, and our voice *should* matter.

Some folks say the Electoral College guarantees a president gets elected, but does it guarantee the *right* person wins? It often feels like our votes don't count, like something's being taken away. The popular vote should be king! Remember 2000? Gore won the *most* votes but lost! The article mentions a poll from back then, saying most people would rather vote directly. If our votes *really* mean something, let's vote on whether to keep this thing around!

People argue the Electoral College is efficient and stops fraud, but is that *really* true? Every system has problems, but is this one a *good* solution? America's all about listening to the people's votes. We should just vote directly, no middleman.

Basically, the Electoral College is unfair, broken, and goes against what America stands for. It stops us from directly choosing who we want, making us pick electors instead. If you believe in American ideals, you should see how wrong this is. It's a bad way to run elections, and we need *real* control!

Senator, with all due respect,

The Electoral College—why does it still exist? Folks argue it hurts democracy since we technically vote for electors, not the person we really want. Yet, listen—it *actually* works! It quickly picks a winner and gives us a president for all of us.

It’s made for a clear win. A simple popular vote? Disaster! Richard A. Posner points out that winners get way more Electoral College votes than regular ones. Less arguing about who really won. He's right, simplifying everything! Plus, no ties like Nixon vs. Clinton in '92. The Electoral College considers more, deciding a clear winner.

It also makes the president think about everyone. Candidates need votes across the country, not just in their favorite spots. We need a president for every single one of us! Imagine Republicans only caring about the South—not fair! Bradford Plumer says candidates skip states they'll lose. Maybe those states are already decided! Why would a Democrat bother with Texas, a Republican stronghold? Or a Republican in California, a Democratic one? Voters probably already made up their minds! But, Plumer’s thoughts might be old news. Things change! Don’t like the Electoral College? Get current info. Look at recent elections! Public feelings in 2004, 2008, and 2012 depended on events, promises, and young voters showing up. The Electoral College makes the president care about the whole nation.

So, keep the Electoral College! It finds the winner and makes sure the president represents everyone. Electoral College vote counts? Higher, making victory clear. Plus, it forces the president to think about the whole country, not just some areas. Senator, please keep it! It's worked well and should keep doing so.

In the old days, people simply walked to get around. Whether it was for groceries, food, or visiting grandma, walking was the way to go. Now, how did we become so obsessed with cars? Nowadays, numerous cities are aiming to reduce the number of cars on their streets.

Cars have a negative impact on the environment. However, there's reason for optimism! New regulations are being introduced to decrease car usage. For example, in Vauban, Germany, people are giving up cars to help the planet, and they're loving it! Heidrun Walter, a mother from Vauban, stated, "Cars used to give me headaches. I feel much better now!"

Paris, the well-known city, prohibited cars because the air quality was so poor. Consequently, traffic decreased dramatically—dropping by 60 percent—in a mere five days!

Because Paris had the worst air quality in Europe, they were forced to take extreme measures. The car ban proved successful, improving air quality and eliminating smog.

Major cities such as New York have streets congested with cars. Due to the close proximity of buildings, walking or biking is often more efficient than driving. New York boasts impressive bike lanes. Furthermore, carpooling is popular, with people sharing rides to reduce the number of cars in use. This results in reduced pollution, fewer traffic jams, and cost savings on gasoline.

Bill Ford, the chairman of Ford, envisions car ownership becoming inconvenient. His goal is to collaborate with phone companies to create cities where "pedestrians, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses coexist harmoniously, saving time and energy, reducing pollution, and enhancing safety." While cars are useful, completely banning them everywhere is impractical. However, reducing their use is beneficial. An excessive number of cars leads to excessive pollution! How about dedicating a car-free day to explore alternative modes of transportation? Biking is an excellent form of exercise and quicker than walking, allowing you to stay in shape and arrive sooner!

Presidential elections are, well, a rollercoaster, especially with the Electoral College! Should it stay or should it go? It's a blend of votes from us and Congress. Those past elections? Total chaos. And the future? Could be even wilder! Shouldn't we, in a democracy, just vote directly for the president? Seriously, this needs fixing, and maybe it's time to ditch the Electoral College for good.

Here's the odd part: Instead of directly picking the president, we choose electors who do it for us. Plus, even when the popular vote wins, the "winner-take-all" system throws a wrench in the works. According to "The Indefensible Electoral College", this hurts voters. Candidates pick their favorites, ignoring states they can't win, and hyper-focusing on those all-important swing states. Some states get all the attention, while others are completely overlooked. Take 2000 - seventeen states didn't even see a visit! How ridiculous is that?

Don't forget that "disaster factor" either. Bradford Plumer believes we dodged a bullet back in 2000, but the whole system could collapse. Just imagine Louisiana trying to swap Democratic electors to mess with Kennedy. Things can get that crazy! Or, states could send conflicting slates of electors, or we could end up in a tie. Even with minor adjustments, we desperately need a far superior system.

Richard A. Posner defends the Electoral College, arguing it promotes stability and helps smaller states compete with the bigger ones. But that's not balance; it actually favors those smaller states. Posner also suggests voters in swing states get more attention. Plumer disagrees, pointing out that campaigns ignored places like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Many voters in big TV markets didn't even see any ads. Plain and simple: the Electoral College is too easily manipulated and must be abolished.

Look, I'm still saying, "Abolish the Electoral College." People should have the direct say in choosing their president. It's a tough fix, I know, but the current system is undeniably unfair. Some might defend it, but come on! We need a complete overhaul, or at least some serious changes to this thing.

To My Esteemed Florida Senator, This Electoral College, huh? It is truly sparking some heated debates! Certain individuals sing its praises, claiming it handpicks our presidents with impeccable precision. Yet, others are crying foul, demanding reforms to address inherent flaws. Surely, we ought to modernize it to reflect our modern challenges. Shouldn't our very system mirror the genuine will of the populace in a true democracy?

Die-hard supporters argue that Electoral College deadlocks are practically unheard of, with electors reliably adhering to the popular vote. But listen, so what if these issues are rare?! That doesn't make the problem any less serious! Small cracks can widen into gaping chasms, much like a hole in one's sock. Let's fix it ASAP, or risk facing even greater consequences down the line! While electors tend to comply, there's always that slim chance of someone going completely rogue!

Does the Electoral College snatch away your vote's true impact? Sure, you cast your ballot, but the electors ultimately decide the winner. States get a disproportionate number of electors, giving off a strange imbalance of power. Some votes carry far more weight than others, and that doesn't seem quite right! People get discouraged and simply skip voting, feeling like their voice does not even matter. Political candidates only pay attention to swing states, and all the other voters end up feeling completely lost.

Apart from voter disinterest, the Electoral College has significant flaws. Ties are indeed rare, but still possible! In such cases, the House of Representatives steps in to decide, possibly going against the will of the nation. Plus, a candidate can win the popular vote yet lose the presidency. Remember Al Gore way back in 2000? I mean... it is a broken system that needs to be addressed! We must fix it before even more severe problems arise.

Though the Electoral College functions on the surface, it fails to reflect the genuine desires of the populace. Instead of us, the average people, electors make the decision. This presents a significant challenge, especially when we assert to be a government by the people. Surely, there is a much better way to truly express the principles of democracy. Do what is right! Get rid of the Electoral College and push for a fair system that embodies the essence of America.

Okay, let's try this, fingers crossed!

Cars are quick, no doubt, but all that pollution really does a number on the environment. Cutting back on driving helps a lot, and here's the breakdown: fewer emissions, less smog, and resources conserved!

First off, driving less immediately lowers greenhouse gas levels. Consider biking, taking the train, or even walking to work! Cars spew exhaust, turning into harmful gases that wreck the planet. A study (let's call it Source 1) shows regular cars create about HALF of these gases! So, fewer cars, happier planet, end of story.

Also, skipping the car seriously cuts down on smog. Smog is just terrible, harming both the environment and your health! Think of it as super-yucky air that makes you feel unwell. Lots of cars can make smog REALLY bad in certain areas. Source 2 showed that Paris's air was once almost as bad as China’s, and everyone knows China's air is awful. Less driving means cleaner air, it's that simple! Paris reduced smog by 60% in just five days by reducing car use! Clean air is a major victory!

Lastly, driving less saves resources! Source 1 states that if we don’t depend on cars as much, cities won’t need to expand as much. Less land's needed for roads, shops, and houses! Source 4 notes this saves huge amounts of building materials and protects natural resources. Less driving makes life better across the board.

In short, less driving is great for the Earth, good for you, and saves resources for the future.

Okay, lots of folks aren't fans of the Electoral College. But hold on! It has some upsides. The Constitution made it this way for a reason. The goal? Stop either Congress or the regular people from having too much control over picking the president. It's like a safety measure, really. It makes sure that everyone across the country gets to speak their mind, truly. State bigwigs get a real shot at backing what their people want. Yes, people whine, but it's still a good way to pick the President, right?

Presidential hopefuls? The Electoral College makes them work hard! They can't just zero in on one place, like only the West Coast, and think they'll win. Nope! They need to convince people who might not agree with them at first. This is awesome! A President who appeals to lots of people will probably do a much better job for everyone. Plus, all that focus on swing states? It gets people pumped, keeps them informed, and preps them to make a smart choice about who to vote for. Seriously!

And truly, sticking with the Electoral College makes sense. Why? It usually picks the same person that most people vote for! Okay, back in 2000, Gore got more votes in total, but Bush won the presidency thanks to the Electoral College. Wild, huh? But, it hardly ever happens! Before that, you'd have to go way back to 1888! The Electoral College voters? Parties pick them, and they almost always vote for their party's candidate, as they should. Scrap the Electoral College and just use the popular vote? Could make new problems. What about a tie? Uh oh! Super unlikely, sure, but what if? The Electoral College helps when no one gets a clear win.

Some say the Electoral College makes people not even try to vote in states where their candidate doesn't stand a chance, like Democrats in Texas. But every vote counts. It still matters for how a state's electoral votes get handed out. People vote in presidential elections to show support, not just to make the final call. The Electoral College has worked for ages, and it will likely keep doing just fine.

Hoping to help the Earth? Try to drive less! Studies show that about 12% of all greenhouse gasses come from cars in Europe. Giving up your car can be hard. However, so many choices exist! You might carpool, get a ride from someone, take a train or bus, hail a cab, or bike! You have to take action if you hope to save the planet!

Our air suffers due to cars! Some places ask that you pay to drive less. Paris did an experiment. After some very bad smog, they stopped cars for a little bit! This proves change is possible! Beijing was once as polluted as Paris. Now, the government cleaned it up. Bogota keeps 7.2 million people from driving on a "Day Without Cars." Mess up, and you'll pay $25 or face jail. Millions of Colombians "walked, biked, skated, or took buses," said Andrew Selsky, who believes that "This plan might spread." People spend time in parks, and the air is cleaner.

If you drive less, it's great for you! Cleaner air comes with less traffic. Also, issues with your heart and obesity are cut back by walking more! Car companies may be unhappy with this. Car sales might go down, hurting Ford, Toyota, Honda, etc. However, people will still drive. Yet, less car making could boost the economy by reducing gas costs. Get this: Driving is reducing each year! Experts are happy for our Earth, yet car companies get nervous.

Now do you get it? Use cars less! Young folks aren't fond of cars. Youth driving fell by 23% from 2001 to 2009, per a 2013 study. Sales dropping causes worries about the U.S. reaching "peak driving," but experts are happy. What happens now? Will it be good or bad? The future is uncertain! Obama’s plans to cut greenhouse gases look to be working. Studies show that "Americans buy fewer cars, drive less and get fewer licenses each year."

Senator, about the Electoral College, wouldn't a simple popular vote be better? Many people don't vote, nearly half, feeling it's useless, as noted in "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. Politicians focus on only a few states.

"In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23 notes that "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," Senator. Why bother if your candidate has no chance? Also, the Electoral College has been manipulated. Segregationists tried to hurt Democratic Electors to hurt Kennedy, highlighted in "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11.

However, some want to keep it, calling it a compromise by the Founding Fathers for fairness, giving citizens a voice per "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. Maybe it's good because people can influence electors, and history matters, says "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15. "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 says Electors should vote for the winner.

Senator, this winner-take-all system isn't fair! It favors big states, with time and money poured into a few. "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," claims "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13.

Senator, shouldn't we choose the popular vote? People would vote if they knew it directly mattered. Power to the people!

Wow, cars are everywhere, right? Nobody walks or bikes anymore; they all drive. This changes everything, doesn't it? Is this what we really want? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky get it. They want fewer cars for different reasons. Their work shows fewer cars mean more free time, better business, saving resources, and less "car culture."

Are you always late because of traffic? Walking or biking might be quicker! Selsky talked about Colombia's car-free day. Millions "walked, biked, skated, or took buses," so roads were "strangely... free of traffic jams." Less traffic means less waiting! Walking helps the Earth and saves time. Businesses thrive when there are fewer cars. Selsky mentions "new restaurants and fancy shopping areas" popping up, and sidewalks becoming "wide, smooth sidewalks." More walking means more shopping!

Saving resources is a huge benefit of driving less. Rosenthal says cars cause "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-heavy areas." That's a big deal! We *must* drive less because car fumes hurt the planet. Some cities have "no-driving" days. Duffer said Paris had a "partial driving ban" after "days of almost record pollution." Less driving means less pollution and less traffic! Great.

Car culture might be fading. Studies show young Americans drive much less now. Rosenthal mentions a "23 percent" drop in young drivers. Maybe it's tickets, parents, or caring about the planet. Rosenthal said experts think this will cut "carbon emissions and help the environment." So, car companies are changing, and tech is changing commutes.

In short: less driving means more free time, better businesses, saving resources, and less car culture. Next time, think about driving less. It helps the Earth.

Alright, look, the Electoral College? We gotta keep it. Some say it's old, but it's still the simplest way to pick a President. Elections are way easier than trying to count every vote. Fewer recounts! It stops big states from bossing everyone around. Keeps things from going crazy.

Electoral College wins avoid crazy fights. Candidates often get more electoral votes than popular votes. Obama, for instance, got almost 62% of electoral votes, even though about 51% voted for him. States use "winner-take-all," so figuring out electoral votes is easy, even if someone barely wins. A tie? Possible, but super rare. Counting every vote? Chaos.

The Electoral College stops runoff elections. Runoffs happen when no one gets more than half the votes. Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92 only got around 43% of the popular vote, but they still won big with 301 and 370 votes respectively. It gives us a clear winner, so we don't have to vote again. Runoff votes are a waste.

So, the Electoral College stops big states from having too much say. We need to keep it. Maybe there are better ideas, but it's the best system we've got now.

*Snap!* My face got caught by Mom's iPhone. It felt like having superpowers to just hold that small plastic thing. Oh, to be young! I remember lying on my stomach in the kitchen, moving a toy car around as Mom cooked, the garlic smell just filled everything. And finally, my driver's license! A big deal for teens, yeah? But, it's not all great. If we drive less, there's less pollution, and we can all breathe easier.

Now that I drive, I hate the smog that makes me squint when I'm going to school. Ugh, my car makes it even worse! Car fumes cause smog, sending greenhouse gasses into the air. It turns out that U.S. cars cause about half of these emissions (Source 1). Even making gasoline pollutes! It's such a waste and lets out a lot of carbon. So, making the fuel releases greenhouse gasses, and THEN our cars release even MORE?! AP Environmental Science taught me that too many greenhouse gasses are BAD. They trap sunlight and UV rays, and then what happens? Global warming. Also, smog is gross. Paris was suffocating, so they banned cars to clear it. In just one car-free day, the smog was gone! People were breathing easy (Source 2). Smog hurts Paris' tourism. Who wants to see Versailles covered in pollution?

Driving less helps our bodies and minds. In Vauban, Germany, cars are not allowed. A lot of people who moved there sold their cars and are happier because of it! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I was stressed. Now, I'm happier." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that giving up the car helps your mental state. And your body feels it too! People are walking, running, biking, or skating all over the place. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars" every year, encouraging people to walk (Source 3). Walking is so good for your heart! If more places had car-free days, maybe we'd see fewer heart problems.

So, less driving means cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. Next time you go to grab your keys to drive to your friend's house, stop and think for a minute. Think about your health, the planet, and maybe just walk instead.

Elections? Super important! They're not like, a daily thing. Your vote? Seriously powerful. It changes everything for our country. Picking the president *should* be easy: count the votes, boom, you've got a winner! Just vote for who you really want. Easy peasy. The popular vote is way better, no weird Electoral College stuff.

Lots of people think the popular vote is fairer. It's super simple: vote, count, the most wins! Candidates try to go to all sorts of states and talk to people, if they have time. But, Bradford Plumer says the Electoral College makes candidates "skip states they can't win, and only focus on states where it's close." So unfair! Every state deserves to see candidates. Some candidates just ignore whole states! Plumer pointed out that "in the 2000 election, candidates didn't even bother with seventeen states, like Rhode Island and South Carolina. People in 25 big cities didn't see any campaign ads." How can voters know what to do if candidates don't even talk to them?

Plus, the popular vote is easier to understand than the Electoral College, which can be confusing. Popular vote? Super easy: vote, count, most wins. Electoral College? Plumer says if there's a tie, "the House of Representatives decides, with each state voting for the president. The Senate picks the vice president. Each state gets one vote, so Wyoming's vote, even though they only have 500,000 people, is the same as California's 55 reps, who represent 35 million voters." Can you imagine the mess? One vote having so much power! The popular vote is clear: everyone votes, the most wins. No need for representatives to make things complicated.

Basically, the popular vote is the best because it's fair. We pick who we want, and no one messes with it. And it's easier than the Electoral College. If there's a tie, representatives might vote, and maybe they don't even know what's going on. Voting is super important, so let's do it right. We should vote how we want, and not let other people, especially representatives, decide for us. Voting really matters!

Okay, so the Electoral College, how does it *actually* function, right? The Federal Register says this: We vote for these *electors*. Then, these *folks* gather 'round and pick the President and Vice President. After all that jazz, Congress counts *all* the votes. Basically, we're picking people to pick the *President*. It seems kinda basic, yeah? Why the fuss?

Lots of people want to ditch the Electoral College and just use the popular vote system. Bradford Plumer wonders if electors will even vote as we *think* they will. And he doesn't think so! Maybe sometimes, but we're still picking electors and *hoping* they choose right. We *have* to trust them. But, if we voted *directly* for the President, we'd *still* have to believe they'd be *competent*. It's basically the same, just with an *extra step* thrown in, you know?

Richard A. Posner has a *great* point: "Why get rid of something we *already* dislike?" He thinks the Electoral College keeps things fair, like the Senate helps small states. *Exactly*! Big states don't have much say in the Senate. So, with the popular vote, big states get *even MORE* attention because *that's* where most of the people are.

Also, the Electoral College helps stabilize things if nobody gets over 50% of the votes. Like in '68! Nixon got the *most* votes (43%), but not the majority. Clinton was similar in '92. Yet, they won the Electoral College, so no runoff. That was *close*!

I like the Electoral College. It stops Texas from bossing around Rhode Island. Plus, it helps us avoid runoffs. Some say it's old, but I think the Electoral College is a *solid* way to vote.

Okay, so, this Electoral College thing? Seriously, it's just awful – super old, not fair at all, and just plain dumb! People try to say it's good, but their ideas don't make sense. Why can't we just vote? Even Dole knew it had to go! The reasons they say it's good? They're all wrong. Also, guess what? The electors can't be trusted! They say they'll vote one way, but they might not! Nixon, Carter, Dole, Chamber, the AFL-CIO – they all wanted it gone. People think they vote for the President, but they vote for electors! What if there's a tie? The House decides, but it's not even a fair vote!

These electors? They don't really vote straight for the president. Can you trust them? Nope! Could voters pick bad electors? Yes! The Electoral College? It's messed up, and it's easy to cheat. People think they're voting for the President, but surprise! It's not that easy at all. We don't even pick the *electors* directly! Why think they're loyal? In '60, Louisiana almost switched electors to hurt Kennedy. So crazy! It's so easy to cheat. The base is unsteady. "Faithless" electors have broken trust. It could happen again easily. It's rigged! Just get rid of it! Let people vote!

If the electoral vote ties, then the House picks who's President and VP. That's even worse! So unfair. Not a real democracy at all. Each state gets ONE vote. Wyoming has one rep and a few people, but has the same power as California with a bunch of people! Does that make any sense? NO! But that's how it is. People split votes, one party for President and one for Congress. So, the House might pick someone nobody wants. Ties are rare? Actually, no: some votes in '68 could have caused one. Some votes in Ohio and Hawaii in '76 could have too. Little changes, BIG results. "Your vote counts?" That's a lie. This system? I can't believe it's still here. People deserve the same say.

Come on: get rid of the Electoral College. Straight voting is the way to go. So simple! Electors can't be trusted. The House is not fair. Let people vote! Let us vote! A President "For the people" should be picked "By the people!"

Hey, check it out! Driving is a big problem for the Earth. Is there any way we could drive less? The planet is really struggling, and cities are getting polluted so bad. Cars are stinking up everything from here all the way to Europe and even Central America, it's a world issue. Burning tons of gas is basically cooking the planet. What if we just drove a little bit less, though?

So, Paragraph 10 of that "Paris bans driving due to smog" thing starts with "After days of near-record pollution..." Paris, with smog? That's awful! The "City of Love" can hardly breathe! If Paris deals with alerts, what do other cities do? We've got more of everything – factories, cars, you name it! What's our plan? If we don't do anything, it's just going to get worse. Come on, let's do something now!

Paragraph seven from "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," has David Goldberg saying, "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Why do we care more about cars than people? I mean, cars are fast, yeah, but what are we giving up for that? Forget gas; what about our health, though? Our money goes to roads instead of parks, and all there is are malls instead of green spots. Trees are awesome for good air, but pollution's killing them. Smog? No one knew about that a long time ago, but now it's all cars!

Alright, so less driving is a must, but we still can do this. What about days where no one drives? You drive, you get a fine! They do it in Bogota, Colombia. The article even says, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Maybe this could be done here too! Cali and Valledupar are even giving it a go.

Last summer, I went to California, thinking it'd be great. I imagined surfing and cool mountains, the whole shebang. Arriving in LA, I expected a lively city, like Miami. Boy, was I mistaken! Seriously, the awful smog and insane traffic ruined everything. I get that it's normal there, but fewer cars would help a lot. Cleaner air and safer streets? That's a huge win!

Elizabeth Rosenthal noted, "Cars cause 12% of Europe's pollution... it's even 50% in some areas" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). Trucks add to the mess, too. Now that pollution is well known, many people want fewer cars. Countries like France, Colombia, and the US are acting on it. And guess what? It's working! Rosenthal said, "Studies show fewer Americans buy cars, drive less, and get fewer licenses" (paragraph 29). Cars will stay, but even less use helps.

Picture fewer cars in places like NYC; there'd be fewer crashes. The US sees about 500 crashes daily, and the world over 5000! Many die in car accidents, but we can stop this. Sure, bad drivers worsen it. But listen: fewer crashes also mean less pollution. Duffer reported, "Traffic dropped 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Also, Bogota's Car Free Day is inspiring Latin America to cut traffic and boost safer walking.

People now get why fewer cars matter. Less car use equals better air and fewer accidents; that's what really counts. After I left the city and highway, I finally saw California's real beauty!

So, what dirties the air? Cars are a big cause. But, less driving is great! Think about it: you breathe better, save cash, and streets are safer for all. Ready to jump in?

Yeah, tons of cars make pollution way worse. Remember when Paris stopped cars during bad smog? Boom! Clean air, pronto. Also, less driving hurts those big oil companies in the wallet. Instead, think bikes, walks, buses, skateboards, or trains – cooler options!

And get this: your wallet gets fatter! Car tickets cost a bunch. Plus, owning a car – gas, fixes, fines – is super pricey. Driving less means big savings down the road.

Lastly, think about safety. One study said changing how we travel – biking, walking, buses – makes things safer. Simple: less cars, less crashes. Plus, less car traffic is safer for bikers, scooters, and walkers. Fewer drunk drivers too!

Basically, ditching the car is a win-win: cleaner air, more dough, safer streets. So, what'll get you to skip driving once in a while?

It appears many people see cars as totally essential. But while they take us places, they're really bad for the planet. Plus, they somehow make walking or riding the bus seem uncool. But what if we just eased up on driving? That's something to think about! Other countries are already doing it, so it's doable. Fewer cars could mean a healthier planet, nicer neighborhoods, and much better public transportation.

Cars seriously hurt our environment. Check out Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." It's about Vauban, Germany, and how they're trying to cut down on those awful greenhouse gases. The article says cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions. And in the US, where driving is like a national obsession, it's even worse, like 50%! That's a ton of pollution, making cities unpleasant. Cutting back on cars could really lower those numbers. Governments are joining in, too. For example, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," tells about Paris, France, banning cars because the smog was so bad. "After days of crazy pollution, Paris only let certain cars drive to clean things up. People with even-numbered plates had to stay home or pay 22 euros. The next day, it was the odd-numbered plates' turn." And it worked! "Traffic in Paris dropped by 60% after just five days of smog!" Even small changes in how we drive can make a big difference.

Fewer cars also make cities much nicer. Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," talks about how Bogota, Colombia, got better by getting rid of some cars. "The car-free day is part of a plan that started in Bogota in the mid-90s. They built lots of bike paths, the most in Latin America. Parks and sports areas popped up everywhere. They even fixed those terrible sidewalks, making them nice and smooth. Rush-hour traffic is much better, and new restaurants and shops opened up." Cities are now thinking about people on foot, realizing cars can be a nuisance and trying to improve things for everyone. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal discusses the US trying to drive less. Bill Ford, the head of Ford Motor Company, wants to work with phone companies to build cities where "walking, biking, cars, buses, and trains all work together to save time, gas, reduce pollution, and keep everyone safe." Many people are trying to make public spaces better for getting around in an eco-friendly way.

More and more, people are realizing we don't need cars as much, so they're driving less. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal mentions fewer people are getting licenses and buying cars. "America's love for cars is fading. The number of miles people drove peaked in 2005 and has been dropping since, according to Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives." The article also points out you don't need to own a car all the time. "The Internet lets you work from home and hang out online. Cities are becoming more attractive, so people are moving back from the suburbs. Phones and carpool apps make it easier to get around, like shared vans for commuting. So, people who stopped driving because of the recession might not start again." Now, it's really easy to find a car when you need it, so you don't have to own one all the time.

Driving less is a really good idea. With fewer cars, we can deal with big problems like pollution. Cities are changing to make public spaces better for everyone. Cutting back on our car use is really important for making people's work, time, money, and lives better, both here and around the world.

Alright, as a young person, I know the Electoral College has been around for what seems like forever. I can't even vote yet, but I still think my opinion should be heard, right? I think it needs to stay. If we picked the President just by who got the most votes, things would get messy real fast. Some places might really love one candidate, but most of the country might not like them at all. That would be a total disaster.

Picking a leader based only on the most votes could be a really bad idea. Article three, paragraph 20, the "winner-take-all" thing, makes candidates pay attention to important states. These states decide the election, so the people there are really informed. But if we just went by popular vote, anyone over 18 could vote just because of silly reasons. Young people might vote for whoever their friends like, or just because they like the party. People might not think very hard about their choices, but the Electoral College stops that from happening.

Plus, a popular vote might elect someone who is only known in one part of the country. Article three, paragraph 19, says that a President who is only popular in one area wouldn't be a good leader because one area can't elect someone all by itself. Imagine if being popular was all that mattered. For example, Rodney is very popular in the South, but he isn’t getting more electoral votes, he doesn’t need to visit the South. If that’s all it was, voters elsewhere might feel left out, and the President wouldn't care about them. He’d still not be “their president,” even if he won. The Electoral College fixes this. We vote for electors who then vote for the president. Winning the most votes in a state means its electors support them; that's fair. A popular vote unfairly hurts people who didn't vote for the winner.

I know some people don't agree. Article two says, "Candidates don’t visit states they can’t win... Seventeen states didn't even see the candidates in 2000!" Candidates ignore states they don’t think they can win. I get the argument for the popular vote, where candidates would visit every state. However, I don't agree. Just because a candidate visits all 50 states doesn't mean they'll win. The Electoral College makes sure a state's majority matters, not just overall popularity. It’s like high school: the popular kid might not be nice, while some less popular kids are. Choosing a President based on popularity could ruin our country.

Mr. State Senator, the Electoral College is super important. A popular vote could give us a bad president or one who wins just because they are popular in one area. Please think about what I said when you talk about this.

Imagine a much better world with fewer cars. Think about how much healthier and safer our planet could be!

Seriously, imagine less traffic. The air could be so much cleaner! Wouldn't it be great for everyone to breathe easier?

Some people might not like the idea, but driving less really helps us all in the long run. Imagine constantly breathing polluted air – how terrible for your lungs! Plus, less driving means fewer gas station trips. Saving money is great, right? You could spend it on anything!

Cars cost you money! Don't forget about the insurance and gas every week. Even if gas is cheap today, it could suddenly get expensive and hurt your wallet!

So, is driving so much really worth what it costs the planet and your savings?

Wouldn't life be calmer if we didn't rely on our cars so much?

Ultimately, it's your decision, though!

Do you ever think about the pollution from your car? Probably not! You're likely focused on where you're going or your phone, not the environment. That's understandable since environmental issues often seem far away! We usually only notice it when there's a shocking news report about the air.

Sadly, because car pollution builds up slowly, the damage to our planet is easy to overlook.

If this continues, both plants and people could have trouble breathing and end up needing hospital care!

Think about a life without worrying about car payments or constantly needing gas.

Many people and families are stressed about money, struggling with bills and gas just to get to work each day.

Instead of your car constantly using gas, why not walk or bike sometimes? Think of all the money you could save!

Maybe ditching the Electoral College isn't so smart. The Founding Fathers made it so Congress and everyday people would both get a say. Now, people whine it's not fair because the electors are actually the ones choosing the president, not us. We just vote for them. But electors changing their vote? That almost never happens; it's just talk. So, even with the complaints, the Electoral College should stay. It makes things easier, cuts down on votes, and gives smaller states more power.

Okay, the Electoral College can get complicated, like in 2000, but that doesn't happen as often as arguing about who got the most votes overall. The truth is, someone can win the Electoral College without winning the popular vote. In 2012, Obama got 61.7% of the electoral vote but only 51.3% of the popular vote. When a candidate wins big in one state, even a small win becomes huge.

Also, the Electoral College avoids elections where no one gets more than half the votes. Look at 1968 and 1992, when the winners only had about 43% of the vote. That could mean ties and voting forever! But the Electoral College just picks someone right away. Some think it even stops people from not voting in states where their candidate is sure to lose, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. If it's pointless, why bother showing up?

Even so, the Electoral College evens the playing field, giving small states a chance against big states. In 2012, Florida was super close, and its 29 votes went to Obama. Candidates try harder in big states because they matter more in the Electoral College. People complain about the winner-take-all thing, but candidates focus on swing states instead of wasting time where the winner is already clear.

Yes, some might not vote because they think their vote doesn't matter much, but one vote almost never decides an election.
