People vote to show what they believe in, not because they think they are the only reason someone wins. The Electoral College, not just the popular vote, should still pick our president; it's that simple.

Okay, so the Electoral College, really? I just don't understand it. Richard A. Posner tried to defend it in his book, but it didn't change my mind. Think about Florida back in 2012. Obama got all of their electoral votes with just 62% of the votes cast. But what about the 48% of people who voted against him? Their votes were basically worthless! It's winner-takes-all, even if the race is really close. Bradford Plumer gets it—swing states are all that matter. Back in 2000, candidates pretty much ignored seventeen states. Only swing states mattered. With a popular vote, everyone would matter, right? Every single vote counts the same, no matter where you live in the US.

And the Electoral College could cause huge problems. Plumer says that electors sometimes vote however they feel like it. It's not common, but it could actually happen! Just one person could mess everything up. And what if there's a tie? Each state gets one vote? That's crazy! So, the people in big states have the same vote as someone in a tiny state? A popular vote would fix all these issues.

Richard A. Posner tries to make the Electoral College look good, but his reasons don't hold up. He thinks that a popular vote would cause even more disputes? Where's his proof for that? He says that candidates would only visit their home states? Maybe, but they would probably lose the election. He says that the Electoral College teaches voters in swing states? Seriously? A popular vote would teach *everyone*. He thinks it's helpful to big states? Absolutely not. Candidates will go where the most people are, it's that simple. Runoff elections? Okay, he makes a fair point. Those could be a pain. But still, it's way better than being unfair or causing a potential disaster in the voting process!

It's almost impossible to defend the Electoral College. There's a ton of evidence that shows it's both unfair and dangerous. A national popular vote? We need to get it done, ASAP. If you're a fan of the Electoral College? Then you're supporting unfairness, and maybe even a disaster waiting to happen. Nobody actually wants that!

America's all about kicking back, having fun, and chasing dreams, right? And, of course, a fair shake for everyone is a big deal. But this Electoral College thing? Come on! These electors, meant to mirror what the state wants, are actually the ones who pick the president. Is that really what democracy is supposed to be? The debate is still raging. Plenty of folks see it as a flawed system, and it's easy to understand why.

Picture this: You cast your vote with passion. Someone gets chosen to vote for your pick. BAM! They pull a 180 and vote the other way! Now you're stuck with someone you can't stand. How's that fair? Suddenly, those electors seem suspect. Wouldn't it be simpler to tally up all the votes? The person with the most wins, period. No need for this convoluted process. It's actually happened: The popular vote winner LOSES thanks to the Electoral College, leaving your top choice in the dust. Voting should be a breeze. We fought for it.

Beyond just dodgy electors, the Electoral College has other hiccups. Sure, ties might seem rare, but elections have been decided by razor-thin margins. Some argue the Electoral College streamlines things in a tie, but does it really? If everyone votes their conscience, resolving ties should be straightforward. A single elector switching sides can shift thousands of votes. Plus, electors often hog the spotlight when their candidate wins, even when broader national or state factors are the real reason.

So, the Electoral College raises questions about fairness and trust. Regular people have little sway over electors, and the system lends itself to manufactured ties. Given America's commitment to equality, a straightforward, transparent voting system just makes more sense.

Have you ever pondered how much all those cars affect our world? Imagine this: nasty, polluted air and resources vanishing fast! Can you picture a future where breathing requires a mask due to the smog? However, if we chose "green" options and cut down on driving, we could help our neighborhoods and the Earth! Picture fresher air, lively parks, and cool new ways to get around. Seriously, go green and prepare to be amazed!

Ever think about all the cars and the yucky smog they make, especially in big cities? It's so bad! Car fumes get stuck, making the air dirty and hard to breathe. Did you know Paris even stopped cars one time because the smog was crazy bad? They said no cars on certain days, and the air got way better! One time, Paris's smog was almost as bad as Beijing, China! But guess what? After the smog went away, cars came back, and so did the pollution!

Nice towns often have way fewer cars! Take Vauban, a town in Germany where you don't need a car. Get this: about 70% of people there don't own cars, and like 57% sold their cars to move there! Heidrum Walter, a person who lives in Vauban, says, "Having a car was always a pain. Now, I feel way better." Also, Bogota, Colombia, wants to cut down on cars too! These car-free lives are great for us and the planet!

Alright, alright, cars probably won't vanish soon, but good news, there are tons of other ways to travel now. In NYC, renting bikes is super popular! Also, sharing rides is getting bigger to make streets less crowded! Interestingly, recent numbers show that fewer young people in the US are getting licenses and buying cars. Think about a future with fewer cars!

So, using cars less is a huge win for the Earth. It can really cut down on smog, and the Earth will be much greener because of it!

Goldberg, from the car-free group Transportation for America, points out that post-WWII roads became primarily for cars. He argues for fresh plans. Things are shifting; cars, once vital, are losing importance. Ever wonder why fewer people are buying cars?

It's speeding up, like in Germany. Vauban, a "smart planning" example from 2006, was intentionally designed without cars. Now, surprisingly, more suburban residents are walking, biking, or taking trams to work or school. "Smart planning" can really help towns reduce driving. In Vauban, a huge 70% of families live without a car and are happier, experiencing less driving stress. Get this: an incredible 57% actually sold their cars to move there! These car-free towns are springing up, densifying cities and suburbs and slashing travel.

A key reason? We must tackle global warming head-on. Cars pump out a huge amount of greenhouse gases, contributing about 12% globally, and a staggering 50% in car-dependent places like the US.

Paris battled terrible smog. Their solution? Ban cars with even-numbered plates, fining anyone who broke the rules, which actually cut down on traffic. This smog came from cheap diesel. Because it was so effective, Paris then banned odd-numbered plates, recognizing that, as the capital, it was still smoggy.

Cars have completely reshaped our lives. But US studies reveal a telling trend: people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and fewer are even bothering to get licenses. Despite America's love affair with cars, a growing number desire a greener world and see real benefits in going car-free. Think about insurance hassles, monthly payments, endless repairs, safety concerns, and, of course, the stress of driving. Cars are useful, sure, but their downsides are becoming clearer. Fewer teens and young adults are getting licenses, and an increasing number are choosing to skip car ownership altogether. Really makes you ask, why even bother owning a car?

Across the globe, people are seriously weighing the pros and cons of car ownership, recognizing the value of driving less, and ultimately changing how they live. One of history’s most transformative inventions is slowly beginning to matter a little less.

The Earth is changing rapidly! People are working hard to lower greenhouse gasses. A significant way to help is by driving less or not using cars at all. Sounds odd, right? Yet some places are making it work, and it is paying off! They are seriously cutting down on car pollution.

Look at Vauban, Germany, near Freiburg. It has no parking spots, driveways, or garages! Instead, people use a tram to get to Freiburg. Heidrun Walter, a media teacher with two children, loves it. Vauban, finished by 2006, shows a growing trend in Europe and the US: suburban living without cars all the time. This small area lets its 5,500 people easily walk to shops on the main street.

Europe makes about 12% of the world's greenhouse gasses, but the US makes a huge 50%! For example, in Paris, the smog became so bad, pollution was out of control. The city stopped certain cars from driving. One day, cars with even-numbered plates could not drive. The next day, it was odd numbers. If you broke the rule, you paid a fine, but the city gave free public transport all week.

Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day Without Cars" to get people to drive less. Millions of Colombians found other ways to get to work. This city of seven million asked people to try other ways to travel to fight smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus said that even rain did not stop people. That year, Cali and Valledupar, two more Colombian cities, joined in. Enrique Rera, the mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, said, "These people are starting something big, and others are joining in!" Besides the "Day Without Cars," Bogota has rush-hour rules, better sidewalks, and new places to eat and shop.

Vauban's car-free design, Paris's short car ban, and Bogota's car-free day show how we can drive less. People want to change things to lower greenhouse gasses. Car sales in the US are going down, and fewer young people are getting licenses – a 23% drop from 2001 to 2009! The goal? Save resources, lower greenhouse gasses, and make things safer. With cities helping, it seems like we can do it!

Want to drive less? Sweet! For starters, you cut pollution when you drive less often. Believe it, cars cause tons of pollution! Some places report cars cause 12%, while others are closer to 50%! To be frank, driving less makes Earth breathe easier, making it healthier for us all!

Think about this: Ditching that drive to work could lower your stress! Going to work or school every day tires you out. Just thinking about traffic jams can cause stress. It’s interesting, traffic has gotten better in certain areas, thanks to small changes during rush hour. So, trying different ways to travel, or driving shorter distances, can bring peace to our busy lives.

Young people are starting to see that driving less is really great. News flash: fewer young folks are getting licenses. One study shows that about 25% fewer young people drive these days. What does this mean? Maybe fewer accidents and a safer life for everyone, especially our kids!

In short, lots of good things happen when you choose to drive less. From less pollution and stress to safer conditions for young people, picking other ways to travel instead of driving helps make nicer, greener communities.

Hey, listen up! Big stuff is about to happen! These days, both politicians and regular people are trying to drive less. Check this out: there's a town in Germany with hardly any cars. And guess what? The people there seem super happy! But Germany's not the only place wanting cleaner air. Even Paris, France is messing around with this "no-car" idea. Sometimes, Paris stops cars when the air gets too dirty. Get this, after only a week, the air is way better! Both Vauban (Germany) and Paris are trying different ways to use fewer cars. But wait, Colombia has *three* cities doing the same thing! Every year, they have a "no-car" day.

So, why did Vauban, in Germany, make a city with so few cars and parking? Think about it: most suburbs have houses far apart, no stores nearby, and tons of cars. Most suburbs are all the same, right? But all those cars make pollution. Vauban fixed this by making it hard to drive. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." The article says cars make about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gasses. But, in some U.S. places, it's almost 50%! Vauban helps clean the air by having fewer cars. They put walkways and stores before roads, making it easier to walk. The money they save from not building roads goes to other ways of getting around. Rosenthal's article says that usually, 80% of transportation money goes to highways. Only 20% goes to other things.

Paris is really trying to change how people drive. There's so much to see in the city, and you can get almost anywhere on a bike. So, why make the city more polluted by driving? That's why Paris sometimes stops cars from entering. The pollution in Paris is almost as bad as in Beijing, China (which is known for being polluted). Robert Duffer wrote "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." At first, Paris thought about banning cars completely to stop pollution. But, it wasn't going to work because so many people drive. So, they found a compromise. On some days, cars with license plates ending in an even number couldn't drive. The next day, it was cars with odd-numbered plates. This went on for a week. People who broke the rules had to pay 22 euros. Duffer's article said almost 4,000 drivers paid the fine that week! Even with the fines, there was 60% less traffic!

Bogota, Colombia, also limits how much people can drive. It's a city with almost seven million people. Every year, they have a day without cars. People walk, bike, skate, and take the bus. Andrew Selsky wrote "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." The article says the city wants to reduce pollution, traffic, and accidents by having people use taxis, buses, and other ways to get around. After three years of success, Cali and Valledupar (two other cities in Colombia) joined in. Selsky says the car-free day was part of a plan to make Bogota better that started in the mid-1990s. Because of this plan, they built 118 miles of bike paths! Plus, new parks and places to play sports.

Even in the U.S. (where everyone loves cars), things might be changing. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "The End Of Car Culture." She says that in the last 8 years, fewer young people are getting their licenses and driving. Instead, they're walking, biking, taking the bus, or using taxis. As traffic gets worse, these options are often faster than driving. And when people do drive, they're sharing rides more often. This helps lower air pollution.

Lots of people don't like the Electoral College. Way back in 2000, Gallup did a survey, and it showed that most folks would rather just have a straight-up popular vote. Still, we should think about all the sides of this. Maybe the Electoral College used to be useful, but things have changed, haven't they?

Picking electors can be a total pain and take forever with the Electoral College. First, states have to pick their electors, then they have to plan and hold meetings. And on election day, figuring out who can be an elector and all the details can be confusing. These electors then vote for the President and Vice President. After all that, the votes get counted. Since there are 538 members in the Electoral College, you need at least 270 votes to win the presidency. A direct election could make things much simpler.

If we had a direct election, that would really change things. Every vote would matter, and that might help bring states together. People want to feel important. Since the Electoral College isn't very popular, lots of people think a direct vote would be a better way to choose the President and Vice President.

Each candidate in each state has their own list of electors, usually chosen by their party. If someone doesn't like the electors, they might feel like their vote doesn't matter much, like they can't really affect who becomes president.

Basically, people are tired of the Electoral College. It's too complicated and takes too long. Some people even think officials are keeping it around to protect their own jobs. A direct election could fix this mess and make picking our leader easy.

From the start, people have aimed for simple living. They created tools to hunt and made carts. Back then, horse poop was a HUGE problem! It needed constant cleaning. Then, cars showed up. Sounds good, right? Nope, they made smog instead.

Recently, Paris stopped some cars because of bad smog. This messed with Paris's weather. Nights got colder, and days got hotter. Some say it is because of dirty diesel, but honestly, anything that burns fuel makes smog! But check out Vauban in Germany. No cars allowed! People don't use cars because buses and trains are great. People in Vauban are less stressed than those with cars. Kids play safely without cars and might spend less time on screens. Is Vauban too much? Well, Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day every year. Only buses and taxis can drive; if you break the rules, you pay a lot. Even in the U.S., young people drive less. From 2003 to 2009, driving went down by 23%. Driving is less popular as better stuff comes along. Is this a new thing? By April 2013, driving was as low as in 1995, when fewer people had cars. NYC even has carpools, can you believe it?!

Sadly, places like Florida do not have good public transport. Going from Homestead to Miami? That's tough! You have to drive to a bus, then take a bus to a train, take an hour-long train, and then another bus for thirty minutes. That's three hours EACH way. That's thirty crazy hours every week! Driving only takes an hour, which is way better. Even doing errands in downtown Miami or Doral from Homestead can take a whole day by car. Without a car, you waste DAYS, or you have to wake up super early.

Looking at Vauban, people seem happier than drivers. They talk to each other more while walking. The air is clean, not like in cities with smog. Cars cause about 12% of Europe's pollution, but over 50% in the U.S.! If Paris had acted sooner, maybe there would be less smog. After WWII, we put a lot of money into cars. Imagine if we used that money for better medicine or other things. In the U.S., highways get more money than buses and trains. Vauban and Bogota work because shops and homes are close, and the bus routes are good. Getting rid of cars completely might be hard because we depend on them, but we can change little by little.

Yo, peep this! The air quality in our nation's becoming something to sweat over, right? Here's a crazy thought: maybe ease up on all the driving? No joke, it could straight up change the pollution game 'cause cars are like, major players in that mess. Plus, picture more chill time with the crew, sprucing up our neighborhoods big time.

Cars are a major contributor to all the smog. Less time behind the wheel equals less junk floating in the air. Imagine less exhaust, meaning less carbon throwing curveballs at the climate. Guess what that brings? Easier breathing and healthier folks. Places where cars take a backseat? They're already vibing better. Word is spreading to "Car-Free Day", like in Columbia! Less exhaust also means no more smog. Cutting back can prevent crazy pollution in cities like Paris.

But hold up, the plot thickens! Driving less actually opens up doors for more hang time. Think leisurely strolls, chats with neighbors, or leisurely bike rides to the park. In spots that are car-free, like Vauban, people are way less stressed out thanks to less traffic. Fun fact: young guns aren't even hyped about cars like before. They prefer buses or ride-sharing apps, so they can kick it with friends and family more often.

Dig this: the constant driving steals resources from fixing up sidewalks and bike paths. Less driving? Boom! Those paths are fixed quicker than you can say "road trip." In Columbia, "Car-Free Day" got them pumped to build new sidewalks and tons of bike lanes – like, over a hundred miles! They also built parks, fields, and shop spots. Driving less can clean towns, make more parks and fun places. Plus, it stops car crashes and makes safe streets.

So, consider cutting back on driving, for real. It's a game-changer. Cleaner air, better health, and tighter communities are on the table.

Swapping out our usual car commutes for other options? Sounds pretty sweet! Imagine, like, way less traffic! Smoggy places like L.A. and Beijing could, like, actually breathe again. And drivers? Less stressed, more money – who doesn't want more cash?

Smog's a big pain, especially in packed cities. L.A. almost tastes like smog, and Beijing's often totally covered. Cars are a major smog source. Europe's cars cause 15% of the air mess, but in the U.S., it's a crazy 50%! Less cars mean less yuck. Need a car? Try a hybrid like a Prius. Some cities, like Bogota, do "Car-Free Days." People dig 'em, and other cities are trying them since they really clean the air. When Paris had crazy smog, they axed cars (except hybrids and carpools). Shows how bad smog can be.

Driving can be super annoying. Traffic jams always mess up your timing and mood. Drivers get ticked off when someone cuts them off and stress about crashes. Picture ditching that stress! Vauban, Germany, got rid of most cars for easier living. Residents own cars but park them in a pricey garage outside town (like $40,000!). You can walk everywhere – stores, food, the whole deal. Cars are mainly for long hauls. Walking for mail or enjoying nature without cars cuts stress. No dodging traffic to eat! With phones and the internet, staying connected without driving is easy. Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram keep you in touch, so you don't have to drive as much.

Lastly, less cars means more cash. In places like car-lite Vauban, you might not even need one. A decent car can run you $30-40k. What could you do with that dough? A sweet home, a killer trip, or even your kid's schooling. And everything's close enough to walk to. Sounds amazing, right?

Imagine ditching the electoral college and simply handing the victory to whoever racks up the most individual votes nationwide – a recipe for potential disaster, no? The electoral college, complex as it may seem, actually streamlines the pivotal task of selecting our leader. Imperfect? Sure. But arguably far more effective than the alternative.

Now, a popular vote does indeed boast certain merits. As Richard A. Posner points out in "In Defense of the Electoral College," it's electors, not ordinary citizens, who actually cast their ballots ("source 3 a15"). But ponder this: a truly popular vote would empower individuals directly, eliminating any intermediary. And are voters even familiar with these so-called electors? Bradford Plumer observes in "The Indefensible Electoral College" that these individuals are chosen by state parties, leaving voters largely in the dark ("source 2 a 10"). Hardly seems equitable, does it?

Despite any voter disorientation, electors generally remain faithful to their expected roles. What's more, the electoral college consistently delivers a decisive outcome. No agonizing ties here! Posner emphasizes, "The winner invariably garners a greater number of electoral votes than popular votes," thereby transforming even the closest contests into seemingly resounding victories ("source 3 a 15"). See how it all comes together?

Furthermore, the electoral college compels candidates to engage with a diverse spectrum of voters. Posner elaborates, "No single region can dictate the entire election's outcome, thus necessitating that the president appeal to all" ("source 3 a 19"). Success hinges on captivating a wide array of demographics. Swing states, in particular, hold immense sway and harbor discerning voters. Posner posits that "People in swing states wield considerable influence and possess keen awareness" ("source 3 a 20"). Candidates would be wise to take heed!

While the allure of a popular vote may linger for some, the electoral college injects structure into the election process. It fosters stability and necessitates that candidates connect with everyday Americans by venturing into various communities.

Okay, so, the Electoral College, yeah? You always hear about it during elections! It has 538 votes in all, with 270 needed to win. States with bigger populations have more votes. A big state such as California has 55 votes, whereas little Rhode Island only gets 3. Remember back in 2000? Al Gore actually won the popular vote over George W. Bush, but he still didn't win the election. That’s when people started really questioning the Electoral College. Some are looking to get rid of it, but I think it is still important. It's really important to how we select our president. It helps whoever wins to win clearly, elects a president for everybody, respects all voters in swing states, and stops ties.

If we ended it, electing a president would be a total mess. The Electoral College is there so we'll always know who won. If there's a tie, the Senate figures it out. Every senator casts one vote, so even smaller states get a chance to say something. Also, candidates need to speak to many areas of the country. The president has to stand for everyone, not just some people. That's precisely why swing states matter as much as they do. Winning states such as Florida, New York, Texas, and California can really change an election. These states have tons of people that care a lot about voting. In swing states, people pay close attention to what candidates are saying. The Electoral College prevents elections where no one gets over half the votes. It is like when Bill Clinton won with only 43% of the vote and still had 370 electoral votes. This keeps us from having to vote again. So we vote for electors, which some may not like, but it makes sure someone wins fair and square, which is what really matters.

So, yeah, keep the Electoral College! It has 538 votes total (and 270 are needed to win) and has rules to break ties. Because of that, we always get a clear winner, it picks a president for everyone, respects all the voters in swing states, and stops elections from ending in a tie vote.

It's obvious that vehicles matter immensely. Using cars less helps everyone, including the planet. If cities are designed in a smarter way, it could really make a difference by resulting in cleaner air, streets that are less dangerous and quieter, and a more chill vibe overall.

Cars are responsible for a sizable chunk of Europe's greenhouse gases, about 12%. But get this: in some U.S. areas where people rely heavily on cars, that number can jump up to 50%! Basically, cars are big-time polluters, especially in cities packed with people. One brainy person said that future suburbs should copy the layout of Vauban, not only in rich countries but also in less rich ones. Middle-class people own more cars, which only makes the pollution problem even worse. Everyone seems to agree that fewer cars equals less air pollution. For example, Paris put limits on driving after there was super bad smog to try and clean up the air. Fewer cars in cities means less smog, which means cleaner air for both people and the planet.

Unfortunately, car wrecks happen a lot, and people die because of them. Sure, you can't stop every single crash, but you can stop a lot of them. Just think about how many people die in busy cities or because people get mad while driving! One driver said it best: "I used to be super stressed when I was driving, but now I feel way more relaxed." Driving can make people feel stressed. When people drive less, there is less stress for all. Think about a better, nicer place where kids are playing and people are riding bikes instead of hearing loud horns, engines, and crashes.

Roads take up tons of space. If more people walked, rode bikes, and used cars less, we wouldn't need so many roads. We could have bigger sidewalks instead. Some cities are really focused on getting people to drive less. Some are “…trying their best to make suburbs smaller…” If stores are all close together on main streets, people can walk there instead of driving to malls. This makes things safer for everyone.

In the end, it's a good idea to use cars less because this reduces pollution and accidents. It also makes our neighborhoods better and quieter. If cities and countries make it a priority to rely less on driving, the world will be a much better place. Our daily lives will also get a whole lot better.

Are we perhaps relying too heavily on cars these days?

Recall when Paris prohibited cars because of awful smog, as pointed out in Source 2? The air quality was so poor that only specific vehicles were permitted! The smog lingered for days, nearly setting new records! Too many cars contribute to smog and harm the environment. However, we have the power to change this! Reducing our driving offers numerous benefits.

What if we could live without cars? VAUBAN, a German town, actually did this! They aimed to discover if a car-free lifestyle could boost happiness. Commuting to work can be stressful due to traffic, noise, and accidents. According to Sources 1 and 3, "70% of families don't own cars, and 57% sold their vehicles upon moving there. 'I used to be stressed with owning a car. I'm happier without one now,' stated Heidrun Walter." This suggests that ditching cars can lead to increased well-being.

Improved air quality is another significant advantage of reducing car use. When air pollution spiked, Paris banned cars to combat the smog. Nearly 4,000 drivers received fines, and some even temporarily lost their cars for contesting the tickets. The outcome? Car traffic in Paris plummeted by 60% within days. The air quality improved, prompting France to allow more cars on certain days.

Are Americans growing weary of cars? Or are new technologies minimizing our need to drive? Elisabeth Rosenthal notes that "the internet enables us to work remotely and communicate online. Cities are improving, making suburbs less attractive. Furthermore, phones and ride-sharing apps offer convenient ways to travel, including vans for work" (source 4, 35). Technology is helping us reduce meetings by using apps, calls, texts, and video conferences.

Reflecting on all of this, I believe that driving less benefits us all. I hope you share this belief!

Cars, cars, everywhere! Beep, beep, honk, honk – red, blue, black, they’re always there. Sure, they get us places. But what if poof? Gone! Could saying bye-bye to cars actually be…good? Turns out, yeah, it just might make the Earth and our brains smile.

Less cars? Earth cheers! Cars drink gas like water and cough out tons of yuck. Europe? Cars cause 12% of the bad air. But hold on, some U.S. cities? A crazy 50%! Paris even said "no" to cars to beat smog. And boom! Traffic went down, down, down – 60% in five days! Less cars, less yuck, happy Earth. Also, young folks drove less from 2001 to 2009, like 23% less! Smart people think this will keep up, meaning even less yuck. Cars cause a BIG mess in the States.

Wait, there's more good news! No cars could make us feel awesome, body and mind. Imagine Vauban, a German town with barely any cars. One person said they used to stress about cars, but now? Super happy. Less traffic and angry drivers? Less stress! Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars," and everyone's happy. One person felt super calm. Less stress? Better brains! And get this: in Bogota, that day makes folks walk, bike, or bus it. Moving makes you happy! Your brain sends good vibes. Less cars, more moving, healthier, happier us.

So, bottom line: bye-bye cars, hello happy Earth and people! Cars are useful, sure, but they cause problems. Next time you grab those keys, think twice. Think fresh air. Think less stress and anger.

Ever wonder why traffic turns into a parking lot, particularly around 5 PM? It's obvious: we drive too darn much. Seriously, fewer trips in cars? That's a complete win and makes life significantly better, period.

Sure, cars might appear like a quick solution initially, yet that apparent quickness doesn't come close to balancing out all the terrible negatives. Major bummer? Constantly being in the driver's seat turns us into total coach potatoes. A walk or run rocks for exercise, but we completely lose that benefit when always stuck behind the wheel. My buddy Walter admitted owning a car used to cause him crazy stress, but he felt way better once he sold it. We absolutely need to take it easy on the driving and look for simple, fun options to get around. Yeah, a walk may take a little longer, but it's totally worth it. Ditch those awful traffic frustrations, and say hello to locations cars simply can't reach. Plus, you can discover secret spots, not just the same old roads. Rain might mess with your walking plans, but Antanas Mockus, a former Bogota mayor, insisted rain shouldn't keep us inside. Cars keep us dry, without question, but walking is much safer overall. Places such as Germany, Paris, and Bogota want to ditch cars due to all these issues.

On a separate note, reduced driving could mean fewer cases of road rage. Folks tend to get super angry with one another while driving, typically due to being way too stressed. If we lay off driving, we'll all be significantly more relaxed. Car wrecks are extremely serious, and way too many individuals die because of them. Plain and simple, driving less could actually save lives. Cars cause way more problems than they ever fix. Walking and biking? Totally easier and safer, hands down. This would also clear up congestion, making it easier for everyone to get around. Drunk driving is awful and ruins lives. A slightly tipsy walker poses nowhere near the threat of a drunk driver. Seriously, just consider all the downsides of excessive driving.

Finally, Barack Obama made the point that we need to cut back on the driving to tackle pollution. Reports are showing fewer Americans buying cars, driving less, and not getting licenses as often, and that's awesome news. Paris flat-out banned cars thanks to crazy smog levels, preventing them from entering specific zones. Diesel fuel really made the smog issue way worse. In France, 67% of vehicles run on diesel, while only 53% do in other Western European areas. Bogota aims to "make other travel choices more appealing and clean up the air." Violators get fined. They've found out that driving less lowers stress and makes the air cleaner. Bill Ford imagines cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all team up to save time, use less gas, lower pollution, and be safer." These are just a few of the many advantages of easing up on driving. Ditching cars totally, or even just driving less, would be a major game changer. Let's ditch pollution, prevent wrecks, and prevent driving underage. Just think about all the bad aspects of driving too much versus not driving as much.

Alright, so we're still dealing with, like, pollution and the Earth getting warmer. Why hasn't anyone fixed this? Cars? They're a BIG problem. What if people just drove less? Look at these places actually making changes. Like Vauban in Germany: no cars! Then Paris, France? Only some license plates can drive on certain days. Bogota, Colombia is trying too!

Think about Vauban, Germany. No driveways or parking here! You have to pay a LOT to park in garages. Apparently, cars cause about 12% of Europe's pollution. It seems small, but it isn't! If we make car-free zones, pollution will drop since people won't drive as much. One person, Heidrun Walter, said, "I was stressed with a car. Now, I'm happier!" It shows life's easier without a car. Less money on gas and repairs means less money stress. Expensive parking can save the Earth and people money.

What about Paris, France? Mondays: odd plates, Tuesdays: even plates. Pollution was worse than Beijing! But guess what? After this system, pollution dropped by 60% in FIVE DAYS. Seriously? These car rules work, and they get quick results!

Also, "no car days" are in Bogota, Colombia. Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "It's good to reduce stress and air pollution." He gets the link between cars and bad effects. Cali and Valledpar in Colombia are doing this too! Leaders from other countries are watching and impressed. So, this idea could spread! It really makes people drive less.

Basically, cars are a big issue. But people don't think about the pollution when they drive. Some good ideas are: paid parking, odd-even plates on certain days, and "no car days."

This country, the U.S., is ours, every single citizen! We have the power and all the advantages. Isn't it amazing we vote for leaders? Most think it's the best way to pick the President and VP, right?

But, here's a snag: the Electoral College is a big problem. Electors might know the country well, and think they know best, but they often don't get what real people go through every day. Regular folks understand our own lives better. As Bradford Plummer said, "the best arguments for it just don't hold water in reality" (source 14). They might know Washington inside and out, but they miss the struggles many face.

It's super important for everyone to be involved in government. If only the Electoral College picked the President, regular folks would feel left out and ignored. We need folks to work together and keep things running smoothly. Otherwise, things will go crazy! Richard A. Posner points out people will "feel as though their vote counts for nothing, believing the new president disregards them and isn't truly their leader" (source 19). People get mad when their candidate loses NOW; imagine the outrage if they couldn't vote at all?

The Electoral College just isn't fair. Richard A. Posner says some like it because of "its undemocratic roots" (source 17). The popular vote works great, so why make it harder? Change can be good, but not always.

In the end, getting rid of the popular vote would be a huge mistake—totally unfair and bad for the country. It goes against "we the people" at its core.

Even with its problems, some folks still dig the Electoral College. States get votes equal to their Congress crew (House plus Senate is the magic number!). It takes 270 to win out of 538 total votes. Fans claim it nails down a clear winner, makes sure different voices are heard, is a big deal in swing states, gives bigger states a boost, and cuts down on recounts.

In a nutshell, the Electoral College tries to keep presidential elections safe and square. It's got a ton of power, but it ain't flawless. The whole "winner-takes-all" game, where the top vote-getter in a state nabs all the electoral votes, can feel pretty unfair. Candidates might only sweet-talk big states 'cause they're packing more electoral votes. And guess what? You can win the popular vote and still lose the whole shebang if you don't snag enough electoral votes.

One solid reason for sticking with the Electoral College is that it helps us crown a clear winner. This should quiet down squabbles about the popular vote, assuming electors play by the rules. Plus, presidents have to think about what different corners of the country need since no single area calls the shots. People also chat about how it shakes up swing states, handing all votes to the victor. And some say bigger states get a leg up, so candidates pay them more mind. This all makes a tie less likely, saving us from another election.

Despite its perks, the Electoral College ain't perfect. Still, some believe it's the best way to pick the president. They're wary of just going by the popular vote and wonder if voters are clued in enough. Even with election rules, defenders of the Electoral College think it's better for the country. That's why it's important to know why some people back it.

Listen, Senator, about choosing presidents – how about we shake things up? Scrap the Electoral College! Why not just count every single vote? Shouldn't the actual citizens, not some hand-picked electors, decide who's in charge? Just putting it out there.

Even big-shot guys like Nixon, Carter, and yep, even the Chamber of Commerce wanted to trash it. People get nervous that electors could throw a wrench in the works and vote based on their own whims. How messed up would that be?

Remember when Obama crushed Romney? Obama totally cleaned up in the Electoral College, with a 29 vote lead. Just goes to show you how janky the system can be, twisting what the people actually want. A vote for everyone sounds way better.

Okay, yeah, the Electoral College can be a lifesaver when things are neck and neck, like when Nixon and Clinton squeaked by with only 43% of the vote. It kept things from going totally off the rails back then. But it still puts too much weight on stuff that isn't the pure voice of the people. I seriously believe the power should go straight to the American people.

Senator, really appreciate you mulling this over. Do we stick with the Electoral College, or finally jump to a system where the people have the final say? Food for thought.

Dear Senator,

The Electoral College has its fans. Advocates argue it compels contenders to engage with swing states, reaching out to smaller communities (Source 3). However, I fundamentally disagree. I see it as a profoundly unjust method for electing our President.

Source 2 highlights its inequity for voters. Due to winner-take-all dynamics in most states, candidates prioritize tight contests in swing states, overlooking the rest. Seriously, a candidate could lose the popular vote yet still secure the White House through the Electoral College. Voters might be unaware of the candidates' true intentions. This makes you question whether the winner truly cares about the state. Source 3 even notes that candidates require widespread appeal across regions to win. A candidate popular in one region, such as Rodney in the South, wouldn't campaign intensely in those states, assured of victory regardless. If Mitt Romney faced Barack Obama, Obama's popularity in the North would significantly hurt Romney—that encapsulates the essence of the Electoral College.

Ultimately, I maintain that the Electoral College remains detrimental and unjust. Source 2 strongly supports my viewpoint. It asserts the Electoral College is unfair, obsolete, and irrational. The justifications for it are unfounded. In essence, the Electoral College contradicts democracy and is deeply flawed.

Many people find the Electoral College confusing and advocate for its removal, claiming it's unfair. Instead of directly electing the president, "electors" make the choice based on our votes. Critics argue this system disconnects leaders from the popular will. A candidate can win without the most votes, because although electors should follow our wishes, this complex process leads to calls for its elimination.

Moreover, voters often lack knowledge about the electors they choose. Some states receive less campaign attention, hindering informed decisions. Presidential candidates prioritize swing states, seeking attention and votes. Despite presidents needing to address everyone, this focus can marginalize voters in less important states. Even voting for someone means picking electors, and winning the popular vote does not guarantee the presidency, leading to calls for a simpler system.

Political parties select "electors" who pledge support for their candidate. The Electoral College winner can lose the popular vote, though it's uncommon. In 2000, Gore won the popular vote, but Bush became president—the first instance since 1888. Such events could recur, altering outcomes despite the majority vote. The debate is intense because most states award all electoral votes to the state winner. However, it prevents drawn-out elections if no one wins a majority, ensuring a clear victor. People want their votes to count, but that's not always the outcome.

The Electoral College is viewed by many as a complicated leadership selection method. Originally intended to boost voter engagement, it can undermine democracy by diminishing individual vote importance. Informed decision-making is vital in a nation that highly values voting. The system should guarantee that every vote matters.

Dear State Senator, I am writing to express my strong belief in keeping the Electoral College as is. Changing to a popular vote system just feels wrong, plain and simple. The House's role in selecting the president is really important, making the whole process feel more just and well-organized for everyone. Plus, it helps people understand how we actually elect our president, which is a huge bonus!

The fact that the House of Representatives might get involved is a great thing about the Electoral College. Source One, paragraph three, explains that each state gets electors based on its Congressional representation: one for each House member, and two for the Senators. This makes sure lots of people get a say. Also, Source One, paragraph five, notes that "The candidate's political party usually picks the electors," showing it's not just the House. The government plays a really significant role.

Another great part of the Electoral College? It feels fair. Source Three says, "The Electoral College fixes the problem where big states have too much power because the Senate isn't fair..." This helps people trust the presidential election. Source Three also points out, "If everything else is the same, big states get more attention from presidential candidates than small states..." The Electoral College really helps out the bigger states as well.

And most importantly, the Electoral College keeps us safe from election problems, which is what matters most, in my opinion. Source Three, paragraph twenty-two, explains, "The Electoral College stops problems in elections where no candidate gets the most votes." This makes voting simpler and easier to understand. The same source and paragraph also say, "We don't need more elections when no one wins the most votes; the Electoral College makes sure there's always a clear winner..." That's a relief!

Okay, so some folks might not like the Electoral College if their candidate loses, but that's no good reason to ditch it. It keeps presidential elections in order. Source Two, paragraph Ten, states, "With the Electoral College, we don't vote directly for the president, but for electors, who then choose the president." Some might feel the Electoral College is unfair. But electors vote for their state's pick, which is fair enough. Source Two also notes, "Basically, the Electoral College is unfair. Because of the winner-takes-all system, candidates only care about close races in 'swing' states." Even with these issues, the Electoral College does more than just count votes; it helps our government pick the best president, as I said earlier!

Basically, I'm totally in favor of keeping the Electoral College. It's good for both citizens and the government. It makes elections fair, stops chaos, and most importantly, cuts down on problems so people are happier with the outcome. Each year, the Electoral College makes elections easier to understand and more organized. Even though the government is very involved, individual votes are still really important. They are crucial to the Electoral College, and so are government votes.

Is the Electoral College even important anymore? It was helpful a long time ago when states had trouble counting votes easily. People voted by state, which was okay. But now, with better tech, it seems slow and unfair. If you're a Republican in a super Democratic state, your vote feels pointless. Wouldn't it be better if everyone's voice counted with a national vote? Also, some states have way too much power because of the winner-takes-all thing. Small states don't get enough attention, making it hard to know who to vote for.

It all started way back when the telegraph was brand new! They needed a different way back then, instead of states counting votes and sending people. Just think about getting votes on horses! So unsafe! Papers could get lost, people could get hurt, and some places might not be counted. The Electoral College made sense then. But like the telegraph, it's outdated. Now, phones can hold lots of votes. Computers can do math fast, giving us answers quickly. We can send votes quickly and fix mistakes fast. What if we used a computer to count everything? Each place sends info to one computer, with checks and balances. Use many computers to stop cheating. It's sad that some people don't want to let go of the old ways! So old and not good!

It's just not right. A Democrat votes in a very Republican area, but the Republican always wins. That Democrat's vote is like it never happened. Gone. Every time. But a national vote would make those votes count. They would actually mean something! The winner-takes-all thing makes it even worse. Winning California by a little bit means getting ALL their votes, ignoring what half the people think! Not fair. Big states rule because they have more votes. What about the small states? Not much attention. Politicians like the big states more, ignoring the small ones. Voters in places like the Dakotas might not know much and just pick a party. The main battles are in the big states. The ads aren't always true, but voters figure it out. Swing states get lots of information. Small states? They are forgotten, so voters don't know as much, and their votes might not matter at all. A national vote would mean candidates would go everywhere, no more big-state bias, and voting would be fair for everyone.

So, here's the thing: The Electoral College was okay back then, but now? It's old and unfair. We can talk and travel easily, making it pointless and not good for our democracy. It's time for a change!

Worldwide, more and more people are giving up driving. This benefits the environment and saves money. Many people desire to help out, even if on a local level. Countries such as China, Spain, and the U.S. are seeing a decline in car usage over time. While some disagree with this trend, others welcome it due to concerns about pollution. If everyone decreased their car use by 50%, imagine the impact on air quality! Cars may be convenient, but they cause several problems.

Visit Vauban, Germany, and notice the minimal car presence. Car ownership is allowed, but parking is tough and costly. It may cost up to $40,000 just to park near your own home! As a result, most people keep their cars outside the area. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article titled "Life Goes On Without Cars in German Suburb," stating that around 70% of families in Vauban do not own a car. Furthermore, over half of the residents sold their cars upon moving to Vauban. Reducing your car usage can have advantages and disadvantages. However, the savings can be significant, with fewer car payments, parking fees, and high gas costs. Plus, the air will also be cleaner.

Paris, France, faced severe pollution issues, prompting the ban of certain cars. According to Robert Duffer, cars with license plates ending in even numbers were temporarily prohibited from driving. Violators faced a €22 fine. The following day, odd-numbered plates were banned. This approach reduced pollution levels! Great, especially for a large city. The primary objective was to improve air quality and facilitate easier breathing. Duffer noted that "4,000 drivers were fined" and authorities "impounded 27 cars." Traffic decreased by 60%! Once the air quality improved, the ban was lifted.

Bogota, Columbia, started with a small idea that became extremely popular. As written by Andrew Selsky, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Despite its modest origins, the concept has gained widespread acceptance.

Once a year, citywide car usage is prohibited! Transportation via buses and taxis is permitted. Around seven million people seek to improve air quality. The goal is cleaner transportation with significantly less pollution. Violating the rules results in fines! Selsky mentions that Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" Parks have improved, sports have become more environmentally friendly, and the air has become fresher. It's so well-received that other cities have followed suit.

Rosenthal also talked about the U.S. She wrote that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. This is consistent with "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. In New York City, taxis and subways are readily available. Parking is challenging, encouraging people to avoid car ownership. Think about the money saved! While traffic remains heavy, imagine the extent of the congestion with more cars. This illustrates the city's dependence on public transit. However, public transport can be costly, and without it, the air quality would be awful.

Therefore, reducing car usage offers broad benefits. It addresses pollution, improves air quality, and saves money. When possible, walking is a beneficial choice for personal health, finances, and environmental well-being. Though driving is sometimes quicker, it is important to consider the related expenses and pollution. Consider reducing your reliance on cars to see the positive changes and benefits.

Car sales are through the roof, and companies are making a killing! Still, maybe we should slow down on all this driving around town. Frankly, studies prove that too much driving boosts stress. As Carlos Arturo Plaza mentioned in Andrew Selsky's "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," driving less helps people relax. Driving less is good for you, and it's GREAT for everyone else! Many Americans drive, yet fewer are buying cars – isn't that odd? Perhaps they are thinking about carpooling; who knows! Sure, the bus might be slow, but it's still a solid option! Seriously, reducing driving in our areas could bring amazing results.

Cars are big polluters, and they drastically ruin the air and ozone layer. We all like how easy cars make things, and getting that license at 16? Sweet! But, we often forget the hassles of owning a car, like ALWAYS needing gas. Can you imagine if we drove less – things would be SO much better! Less driving aids the ozone layer, and we get to breathe easier. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," perfectly shows how well these bans work. Paris is super smoggy, so they banned driving to clean up the air. Cold nights mixed with warm days trap those nasty car fumes, worsening pollution, which led to the ban. Afterward, the air in Paris significantly improved. The article pointed out, "Paris typically has more smog than other European capitals... Last week Paris had 147 micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with the 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London..."

Look, nobody's saying you should trash your car for good, but consider the downsides. You might find it's more trouble than it's worth. Vauban, Germany, gets it, and they've completely changed their lifestyle. Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," details how the town functions without cars. Folks there say they're happier without all the traffic and crashes. They did it by ditching street parking, driveways, and garages – who needs 'em without cars? Instead, Vauban improved sidewalks and ease of movement. A Vauban resident, a media trainer with kids, shared, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Previously, the government spent 80 percent on highways and just 20 percent on other ways of getting around. But, things are changing over the next six years, showing they're planning better.

Therefore, we really need to rethink how we get around cities. Less driving leads to great stuff: the government can spend money elsewhere, the air clears up, we feel better, and we save money. Is all that driving REALLY worth the problems it causes? We need to seriously ask ourselves that.

The United States roads? Swarming with vehicles! While everyone enjoys driving at high speeds, few seem to appreciate walking. Here's the dilemma: with everyone behind the wheel, congestion skyrockets, prolonging travel times immensely! Moreover, vehicles harm the environment and pollute the atmosphere. Diminishing the number of cars? Fantastic! It benefits both the planet and ourselves.

Reducing reliance on cars? A true blessing! It enhances the planet's health and uplifts our spirits. Shockingly, a study revealed, "Vehicles are responsible for 12% of Europe's pollution... even surging to 50% in the U.S. sometimes!" Considering cars emit harmful fumes, opting for carpooling or public transport is highly beneficial. Plus, walking or cycling? It keeps us in shape!

That repulsive urban haze, smog, originates directly from car emissions. It's laden with unpleasant substances that induce illness and exacerbate asthma, especially in urban areas. Take Beijing, China – a city notorious for smog. "Paris even prohibited cars to purify the air." Indeed, Paris took action to resolve the issue. A decrease in cars equals a "clean air" solution.

But there's more to consider! Vehicles also impact water and land. They consume gasoline extracted straight from the earth. Extracting that gasoline? It can devastate nature, resulting in oil spills that decimate marine life. Therefore, hop on trains, buses, or embrace walking/cycling instead! By doing so, our gasoline demand diminishes significantly. Since cars devour fuel, fewer cars translate to a substantial reduction in overall fuel consumption.

Individuals assert that life becomes simpler and more peaceful without a car. No more traffic gridlocks or accidents! During a "Day Without Cars" event in Bogota, Colombia, Carlos Arturo Plaza exclaimed, "'Less stress and cleaner air, hooray!'" See? Fewer cars equal reduced stress. Heidrun Walter noted, "'I was stressed with a car. Now I'm much happier!'" She thrives in a German town where cars hold less significance. Reduced stress? Another compelling reason to ditch the car permanently.

Some individuals genuinely require a car when buses and trains simply won't suffice. As someone remarked, "They walk, take the bus, or share cars," and "'A car is reserved for truly challenging journeys.'" By avoiding unnecessary car trips, we can foster an improved system where car dependency decreases, and alternative modes of transportation flourish. Better buses? Lessening car usage is advantageous for the earth and our well-being.

Decreasing the amount of cars? It benefits the earth and promotes relaxation. One source emphasized, "Cars rank as the second-largest source of pollution in the U.S.!" By forgoing drives and opting for buses instead, we can drastically reduce pollution levels in America. Reduced pollution, healthier populations, and less stress? All thanks to fewer cars on the road!

Turns out, ditching the car? Pretty smart! Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, a German town, in her article. Vauban's the kind of place where cars are basically nonexistent. Heidrun Walter, a resident, says life's just plain better without them. Instead of noisy cars, you hear kids giggling and bikes whizzing past! The streets are bustling, peaceful, and super clean.

Here's the thing: less driving could totally transform our suburbs for the better. Rosenthal's article shows that cities have been getting denser and more pedestrian-friendly for two whole decades! The idea? Smaller suburbs that are easy to navigate, with great buses and trains, and shops right around the corner – so we don't have to rely on cars so much. Even the EPA thinks it's a fantastic idea!

The payoff is huge: cleaner air when we leave the car at home. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," talks about how Paris tried to tackle pollution by banning certain cars. And guess what? It actually worked! Traffic plummeted – a whopping 60% – after just five smoggy days. Before, the pollution was as bad as Beijing, China, which is known for its terrible air. Paris proved that less driving equals less pollution and traffic jams.

Adding to the evidence is Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota." It highlights Bogota's car-free day, which encourages people to explore alternative transportation and reduce smog. The outcome? Brand new parks, sports areas, improved sidewalks, restaurants, and charming shops. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a local business owner, now finds biking with his wife less stressful and more environmentally friendly.

Bottom line? Less driving is a win-win for everyone! Less smog, less pollution, and easier access to local shops. It improves our lives right now and helps protect the planet for future generations.

Wheels, a game changer, fathered the auto. Currently, manufacturing plants crank out vehicles annually, drastically enhancing transit, near and far. Yet, gas guzzlers emit high volumes of CO2, damaging the Earth with ozone deterioration and corrosive acid rain. Furthermore, extreme car usage promotes gridlock and other issues. Vehicle ownership is also very costly. To conserve the environment, it is important to make and drive fewer cars.

Indeed, automobiles generate substantial pollution through CO2 emissions, consequently quickening global warming. As mentioned in Article 1, paragraph 5, "Automobiles are responsible for 12% of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... however, it could rise to 50% in the U.S.!" Producing and utilizing fewer automobiles would benefit the environment. Reduced global warming promises saving polar regions and creatures like penguins and polar bears.

Traffic jams often surface unexpectedly. As emphasized in Passage 2, paragraph 14, "Paris transit diminished by 60%" by restricting specific license plates on select days. Easier transit will be more favorable! A prolonged three-hour delay transforms into a mere twenty-minute commute, affording individuals more spare time. Decreased car use and traffic reduces incidents.

Expenses related to automobiles, regardless of buying or leasing, swiftly drain funds. Debt exists since the beginning, impacting everyone. Article 4, paragraph 32, notes that "Impoverished Americans could not afford vehicles, and those without jobs were not commuting." Overspending on vehicles affects individual finances and the entire economy. Smaller car debt will improve finances for all.

In summary, substantial rationales exist for minimizing car usage. Autos negatively impact the planet through ice melting, ozone depletion, and worsening weather events. Decreased traffic facilitates movement and maximizes spare time. Reducing car manufacturing and leasing could boost the economy. Simply put, minimal car usage benefits the planet and it's inhabitants.

Alright, the Electoral College, an antiquated oddity, sparks considerable ire. You see, it is not a direct presidential vote; it’s a selection of “electors”. Yet, here’s a twist: their vote might betray your wishes! Recall the 2000 election? The Electoral College seated a candidate against the popular vote! Proponents suggest it prevents ties, yet the logic appears flawed.

This ancient system presents issues, epitomized by the 2000 debacle. The election was utter chaos! Abolishing it could prevent a recurrence. Furthermore, in 1960, segregationists nearly swayed Louisiana’s Democratic electors against Kennedy. Electors can flip, which is an abomination. This is a rare incident, however, it's a *massive* issue.

More than peculiar, the Electoral College is fundamentally inequitable. Each state gets a vote, granting someone in sparsely populated Wyoming equal clout to hordes in California! While they boast of averting ties, remember 1979? A handful of Ohio votes could’ve resulted in deadlock! The “winner-take-all” approach leads candidates to overlook states. Some states went unseen in 2000, denying voters even a choice.

Many feel sidelined, particularly after Al Gore’s popular vote win but electoral defeat in 2000. Voters might abstain if their state seems decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. This is not a game; their vote *is* crucial. A popular vote could induce ties, but it's superior to this flawed system. Over 60 percent despise the Electoral College – a *huge* proportion! Change is needed before things escalate.

It's plainly apparent: Failures, close shaves, issues, inequity are on display. Defenders of the Electoral College are misguided. The 2000 election and outraged electorate spotlight the madness of sustaining it. The system persists, likely for the foreseeable future. Yet, with heightened awareness, progress becomes possible.

Hey, ditching the car more often? Awesome idea! We score cleaner air, explore fun new ways to get around, and suddenly, our streets feel way safer.

See, fewer cars equal less yuck in the air. That's a win for us and Mother Earth. Vauban, Germany, is killing it with this. Cars spew tons of greenhouse gasses—like 12% of Europe's problem. Crazy, right? The U.S. is even worse, at 50%! Places like Vauban, Paris, and Bogota are making moves to cut car use and clear the smog. Experts think this car-free vibe is here to stay, which is huge for slashing carbon emissions. Turns out, transportation is the second biggest pollution offender in the U.S. Plus, ditching the car unlocks cooler travel options. Forget traffic jams, stroll through parks, play some ball, just get moving! Bogota, Colombia, had a car-free day. Folks walked, biked, skated, or hopped on public transport. The roads were super clear. Paris even banned some cars to clean the air, which caused a 60% traffic drop. Public transport was free from Friday to Monday, showing how less driving benefits everyone.

Heidrun Walter, a car-free queen, says she is way happier without one. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, swears less car use chills him out and helps the air. Also, less driving, less danger. M. Sivak's studies show fewer young adults (16 to 39) even bothering to get their license, while older drivers keep cruising. One study even saw a 23% drop in young adult driving from 2001 to 2009. This shift is a major win-win for everyone. Miles driven are nearly 9% below their peak, and fewer people own cars than before the recession. Because of these changes, people might not be going back to being so reliant on cars anytime soon.

Basically, driving less is a fantastic plan for helping the planet, finding fresh ways to travel, and boosting community safety.

Traffic, am I right? It is such a hassle, no doubt. Whether you drive or not, you are still impacted by it. Cars were once the main option, period. Nowadays, with all of the pollution, fewer people are driving as much, understand? Reducing driving would be great; fewer worries, better air, and overall wellness!

Imagine: significantly less stress without traffic jams and constant rushing. We could bike, use public transport, or walk! Check out Vauban, Germany. Studies indicate it is mainly car-free, and residents seem much calmer because they can easily walk anywhere.

Also, cars negatively affect our environment and badly pollute the air. In Europe, vehicles cause roughly 12% of global warming emissions! In the US, some areas with many cars generate about 50%! Paris shows how awful cars can be; one study showed its air became as polluted as Beijing’s! They even temporarily banned cars to improve the air quality.

Fewer cars benefit us and the environment. Another study showed that Bogota, Colombia, has an annual car-free day. This significantly improves air quality, and people enjoy it! Even in heavy rain, they participate in biking events. It was so successful that two other Colombian cities are trying it. Bogota even has the largest network of bike paths in Latin America, around 118 miles! Besides government action, individuals are driving less too. Reports say driving has decreased by around 9%. This might be because more people are working remotely, reducing their need to drive.

Thus, things are changing, possibly for the better. Cars have less influence than before, meaning cleaner air, less stress, and an improved life for everyone!

In Britain, the party with the most seats chooses their leader; yet, when we vote directly, we all have a say! This is the core difference between direct democracy and the British system. Leaders might be persuasive, but not always great, thus preventing power abuse is vital when we vote. To keep things fair, and ensure the people’s power, the U.S. should retain the Electoral College.

Thanks to our wise founders, the U.S. has long been a republic. The Electoral College stemmed from both Britain AND the Iroquois, though bias obscures the Iroquois influence. Just as British politicians pick the Prime Minister, the U.S. founders sought their way to elect a leader, fearing a President elected directly might become a dictator. So, the President had to listen to Congress, who could remove a rogue President. See the Electoral College as guarding against despots. Take Hitler: never elected. Mussolini rose in popularity. The Electoral College stops this with normal citizens; it's not ideal, but it does represent us. Richard A. Posner called it undemocratic because electors, not people, vote. All governments have flaws, sadly. Despite good intentions, some want to axe the Electoral College. Even Nixon, who resigned in scandal, wanted to end it. Critics like Bradford Plumer see it as dated and biased. Still, the Electoral College keeps elections safe, and electors for each state keep it equitable for all. That's representative voting!

The Electoral College represents us all, thoughtfully crafted by America's founders. Despite discord, admired presidents, like Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson, were all elected due to it. To prevent tyranny and empower the people, the U.S. must keep the Electoral College.

Hey Senator, this Electoral College? Feels ancient, right? Shouldn't we just pick the president ourselves? It seems so unfair, like the system's busted. With all the stress already, we need fairness, especially with this debt. Obama didn't exactly fix it. Where you live shouldn't change your vote's power.

These electors? Too much power! Our votes feel pointless. We pick them, yeah, but they decide. That's a big trust issue with the government. Shouldn't voters decide what happens with the Electoral College? Lots probably agree. Okay, I'm young, but my voice matters.

Plumer's right: we don't vote directly. Remember 2000? Gore won the vote but lost. If we want equality, why special treatment for electors? It makes our votes feel less important. If electors pick the winner, why vote at all?

Some say the Electoral College stops one group from taking over. But winner-takes-all decides it all. So, who are these electors and why are they so special? I'm young, but not dumb. Why ignore me? America feels built on a lie, not fairness. We think we're in charge, but really, politicians and the Electoral College pull the strings.

America will get worse without changes. Ditching the Electoral College? Big step. Why should you care what I think? I'm just a kid. I know what it's like to be ignored, even when right. America knows what's needed. Don't let a few decide everything. Let us pick the president, even just for a try. If it fails, we revert. But we gotta try it to know.

Okay, get this: most people don't think twice about cars, but guess what? They're actually a big deal. We see them all the time, sure, but lots of folks can't afford them or drive them. Take Vauban, Germany. They just got rid of cars! They have a train to Freiburg and roads around the outside, but none inside. You can own a car, but parking? No way. Almost none, and super pricey. A garage spot far away? Forty grand extra! Check it: 70% of people there dumped their cars. More than half SOLD them after moving there! Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mom, said it best: "I was stressed with a car. This is way better." Vauban, since '06, shows us the future: suburbs without cars. Cool, right?

Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote the article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and nailed it. Cars are cool, but HEY, AIR PROBLEMS! They're hurting the Earth! Paris got so smoggy, they banned drivers for a while. One Monday? Even license plates had to stay home or pay up. The next day, maybe odd numbers? The air was so bad because the weather trapped all the car fumes. Crazy! Traffic dropped – 60% in five days! The air was like Beijing. Blocking half the cars cleared the air FAST. Odd numbers drove the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, had a NO-CAR DAY. Walk, bike, skate, bus – your choice! No cars, period. And guess what? ZERO ACCIDENTS! They do it every year. This was year three! They thought seven million people would use something else. It rained, but who cares! People still showed up! Mayor Antanas Mockus said, "Rain didn't stop anyone!" Everyone was relaxed, and the air was cleaner, too. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biked with his wife. "It's good for stress and pollution," he said.

So, in the US, cars are HUGE. But are we done with them? Some think so. We're big with lots of suburbs, so we love cars. Model T, Detroit, "Mustang Sally" – it's American. But maybe we're changing. By 2013, driving was down almost 8%. That's like '95! Money was tight, and fewer people drove to work. But Michael Sivak at Michigan said something else was happening. "Car ownership dropped BEFORE the economy went bad." Wow. We need other ways to get around, so people don't just go car-crazy again. New York's bike rentals, tolls, car rentals? Good start. One study showed young folks drove 23% LESS between 2001 and 2009!

People want fewer cars because they want to be safe, save money, and help the planet.

It seems like everyone is driving a lot these days, going to work, school, or different activities. It feels easy, but it can become a big problem. It might seem hard to change, but doing so can help a lot. The air becomes cleaner, we feel more healthy, and feel much happier. Leaders are looking to cut down on driving for these reasons.

According to Robert Duffer, the air in Beijing was bad in 2014 due to cars (source 2, paragraph 14). Similarly, Paris stopped some people from driving on some days if their license plate ended with a certain number. They were fined if they broke the rule (source 2, paragraphs 10-19). When the air became better, they stopped the rule. It shows that driving less makes a big change! Elizabeth Rosenthal wrote in 2009 that cars make the air worse in Europe and the US (source 1, paragraph 5). This means when we don't drive as much, the air is cleaner and we don't use up as much gas.

Driving to work appears convenient, but think about how much better you'd feel if you drove less often. Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, where a lot of families do not own a car (source 1, paragraph 3). Heidrum Walter lives in Vauban without a car, and says that she's less stressed because of it (source 1, paragraph 3). Andrew Selsky said that cars weren't allowed in Bogota, Columbia, for one day in 2002 (source 3, paragraph 21). Carlos Arturo Plaza lives in Bogota and felt much more calm and the air was better, too. It's obvious that not driving as much makes us feel better. So, we should do this for ourselves!

It's a popular idea to drive less because it is beneficial. The EPA even aids towns that want fewer cars in general. Leaders are helping out as well (source 1, paragraph 9). According to David Goldberg, our post-World War II designs make us use too many cars, so we need to make changes. Mimi Sheller said that things are already changing, so it's just a matter of time (source 4, paragraph 35). Everyone seems to agree that driving less is generally positive. Let's try to drive less often, for ourselves and for the earth!

Things are changing a lot in the world right now! The younger generation drives less than before (source 4, paragraph 41). Now is a great opportunity to take buses, bikes, or trains instead of cars. Not driving as much makes the air cleaner and makes us healthier overall. The EPA wants us to drive less for these reasons, and share this information with the world. This needs to happen to protect the environment and people in the future. Driving less is good for the world. So, let's go ahead and make it happen!

Listen, Senator, if I may chime in here. Okay, so, I'm just a college kid, not some political mastermind or anything, right? But I really think the Electoral College thing is the right path. Don't you think it makes picking a president easier?

See, the Electoral College has 538 of these electors, and getting 270 of their votes means victory. Sure, we vote for electors, whatever, but what we're really doing is showing love for a candidate, believing in their big ideas. The system we've got makes swing states super important, and that's a plus in my book. Like "In Defense of the Electoral College" mentions, those states get more involved. This could, perhaps, keep elections from dragging on forever, and also protect the little guys, the smaller states.

Lots of states have this "winner-take-all" rule, handing all their electoral votes to whoever wins there. There are other options, like "proportional representation," but I like "winner-take-all" better. The Electoral College can seem weird sometimes, but this part makes things simpler, really. Giving all of a state's votes to one candidate makes things calmer, which you don't get with proportional representation, because that could make election results take longer.

Basically, the Electoral College has been around forever. "What Is the Electoral College?" explains that the founders set it up to strike a balance. They didn't want Congress picking the president, but they also didn't want just a simple vote. That's why it's important to remember the history here. Thanks for listening to my thoughts.

Senator, hear me out:

Why not just ditch the Electoral College and let the popular vote decide? The person with the most votes should win, plain and simple! Seems like the fairest way to choose our leader, don't you think? This whole elector thing—voting for electors who *then* pick the president—feels kinda fishy, if you ask me. Shouldn't everyone have a say in who's in charge, not just the "insiders"? It's time for a voting system makeover.

Honestly, what's the point of electors anyway? "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2) nails it—the system's totally stacked! Candidates just camp out in swing states. But then, "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) claims no single region can win alone. Yet, the Electoral College basically sidelines some places; their votes don't even count, so the president can just ignore them.

And another thing, *everyone* should get to vote, not just citizens. Excluding people just because they're not citizens? Totally unfair. Some areas constantly get the short end of the stick with electors, making certain voices louder than others. Source 3 argues voters in swing states care more 'cause their votes actually matter. But, seriously, is it right to favor "thoughtful voters" when others want in on the action too?

Alright, I admit, the Electoral College *does* have some perks. Voting for electors is pretty easy, I guess. And if there's a tie, the House decides, with each state getting a single vote—so at least there's a backup plan. Sure, the popular vote might have its downsides, but we can work through them. Electoral College can also shortchange some areas in terms of electors.
