Elizabeth Rosenthal, writing in "The End of Car Culture," says, "If this keeps rolling...the planet will be in better shape, since cars are such big polluters in the US." We Americans buy way too much stuff, and that cranks up pollution. We want the latest and greatest gadgets, which costs way more than just money. Greenhouse gasses are spiking, so we gotta switch gears. Rosenthal points out that "President Obama set some major goals to slash America's greenhouse gas emissions last week." In car-free zones, it's not just about the air. Andrew Selsky spilled the beans about "the construction of 118 miles of bicycle paths" and "rough sidewalks swapped for smooth ones" in Bogota, Colombia. This makes walking way more appealing. The writer shares that "officials from other countries swung by Bogota to soak in carfree day and were super stoked," including the mayor of Paraguay's capital. Seeing positive vibes inspires others to jump on board. Columbia's event has sparked convos nearby, with "two other Colombian cities, Calli and Valledupar, joined in." Even with all this, car companies are still growing. Rosenthal explains, "But it could hurt the car business. So, companies like Ford and Mercedes are rebranding themselves as "mobility" companies that do more than just make cars." With walking, biking, and buses gaining traction, car companies are hustling to hang on to customers, even without the cars. She tosses out Bill Ford's idea of car dealerships "work with phone companies to build cities where 'walking, bikes, cars, and buses all work together to save time, use less stuff, pollute less, and be safer.'" It’s all about combining transit with tech for a win-win.

Without a doubt, things are shifting. Cities are playing around with fresh ways to cruise around or spiffing up the old ways. Henry Ford might be bummed that cars are losing popularity, but Mother Nature is stoked about our green moves. The future's still up in the air, but we've only got one Earth, so let's treat her right.

The cry to get rid of the Electoral College is strong, just like it was back in 2000. Many view it as wrong, useless, and deserving to be gotten rid of.

But wait! Richard A. Posner had a different view. He believed it stops "elections where nobody wins by a large margin." It gives us a clear victor, stopping endless runoff elections. Posner also noted that the winner usually gets way more Electoral College votes than simple votes, lowering the chance of a big fight over the results than if we just counted every single vote. Still, the main point is: people should elect their president. So, the Electoral College seems like a chaotic mess, which makes folks want to get rid of it.

A big worry is that the person everyone voted for might not win, meaning the country doesn't get who it really wants. Bradford Plumer says the Electoral College means "the popular vote winner [could lose] again." This reduces people's power, and that's bad for a democracy. Plumer also mentions that voters can't always "control how their electors vote," so they don't have as much say in who wins. This hurts the idea of the people ruling, so it's a good reason to get rid of the Electoral College quickly.

A further problem is how much power those electors have. Plumer notes that "electors could always go against what the people want," showing how important they are in choosing the president. That power should be OURS, the people! He explains how electors "have sometimes voted against their party and picked whoever they wanted," showing they can do their own thing. The popular vote is all that should matter. America should go where its people want, and that means directly electing the president.

In short: The Electoral College isn't fair to Americans. The right to choose our leader must be protected. The popular vote has to win, and we need to get rid of the Electoral College for good.

Every day, people hop in their cars to commute to work or school. But guess what? Things are changing! More and more, folks are choosing to drive less, which is fantastic news! It's a win-win: good for the Earth, and a stress-reliever for us.

Less driving means less pollution. And that's awesome for our planet! Cars spit out fumes, which create smog, trapping icky stuff in the air. Ever notice smog in big cities? Take Paris, for example: at one point, they had 147 micrograms of air pollution! But when they limited car use, the smog disappeared almost instantly – bam, gone in a day, and never came back! Cars are major producers of greenhouse gases: 12% in Europe, and a whopping 50% in the US. So, yeah, cars aren't exactly Earth's best friend. Even ditching the car just one day a week can make a real difference. Even Americans are catching on! Fewer people are buying cars or driving as much. Since '95, driving is down nearly 9%. By driving less, we can curb pollution and make a huge difference.

Beyond helping the environment, skipping the daily drive can lower stress. In Vauban, Germany, almost nobody owns a car – only 30%! Some might argue families need minivans, but Heidrun Walter is perfectly happy car-free. In her words, "When I had a car, I was super stressed. Now I feel much happier and relaxed." Bogota, Colombia, has been running a "Day Without Cars" for three years now. Only taxis and buses are allowed. Even in the rain, people love it! Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza chimed in, "This makes me relaxed and cleans the air." Now, two other cities in Colombia are looking to do the same. Technology lets us work remotely, chat with friends online, and cut down on driving. That's a major stress reliever!

We used to think cars were the only way to get around. But we didn't always consider the harm they cause to the environment or our health. It's time to rethink our habits and learn more about our options.

The Electoral College? A terrible method for choosing our leader, truly failing the voters. Sometimes, state electors flip the script, ignoring the popular vote. Remember Al Gore? Most votes, but lost! Simply ridiculous! This old setup has serious issues.

You think you're picking the president, but really, you're choosing "electors." Supposedly, they vote for the winner, BUT, surprise, they might not. A candidate can win the most votes overall but still lose the election. No electoral votes. How unfair! The people should always win. Back in the day, electors helped the ignorant, but nowadays, anyone can choose a good leader. Gore winning the most votes but losing in 2000 shows what's wrong with it all.

So, why is the Electoral College so bad? It's the "winner-take-all" thing. Win a state? Even by a tiny bit? You get ALL the votes. Candidates skip small states, ignore safe wins, and only focus on Ohio, or whatever swing state is undecided. Smart? Nope! Back in 2000, 17 states? Ignored! Voters? Uninformed! Uncared for! Many votes don't count in close races. Think California. Almost half of 35 million votes are pointless! Elections should not hang on Ohio only. Other votes matter.

We need something better, definitely. The people's choice wins if we just had a popular vote. The leader has to hear everyone, and listen to them, even the rural voters! American citizens should have elections that are fair and mirror what the majority want.

The U.S. uses the Electoral College to elect presidents, which was the plan from the start. When you vote, you’re really picking electors. These electors then cast votes for president and vice president, and Congress tallies these votes up. Some people support this system because they think it makes for a clear winner. But, others dislike it, saying it favors large states and can cause strange outcomes. This all-or-nothing system doesn’t please everyone.

A state’s elector count matches its number of Congress members, meaning more people equals greater influence. Take Hawaii, with just four electors, versus Texas, which has a whopping thirty-eight! Because of this, candidates chase after voters in big cities. Bradford Plumer noted that Wyoming has “one vote per 500,000 voters, mirroring the impact of 55 folks in California with 35 million voters. So, the final result might not mirror what the entire country wants.”

Picking presidents in this way can be complex. Back in 2000, Gore got more votes from people but lost the election. And can we really trust electors to keep their word? An elector changing their vote could alter the election’s outcome. Plus, way back in 1960, Hawaii presented two separate lists of electors.

The winner-take-all system creates a big problem. Getting the most votes in a state hands you all of its electors. As a result, candidates ignore states they don’t expect to win and concentrate on “swing states”. Richard A. Posner said, "During 2000, 17 states didn't see any candidates, like Rhode Island and South Carolina. And, folks in 25 big media areas didn't see any campaign ads.”

In conclusion, the Electoral College has issues. It makes things complicated and can feel unfair. Even Nixon and Carter wanted to get rid of it. Candidates focus too much on a few states, electors could switch their votes, and the winner-take-all rule isn’t equitable. Simply stated, the Electoral College muddles the process.

Okay, so, no more car? Hard to believe, right? Sure, lots of folks have them. Seems weird to ditch yours. But, maybe it's not so bad? Could be a change for the better!

Cars are super polluting. Robert Duffer points out that in France, "67 percent of the cars are Diesels..." (Duffer 16). Why's that? The government charges less tax on diesel. More cars just mean more jams. Rosenthal even says, "New York's bikesharing and rising bridge tolls show these new goals. Every four years in the U.S., we vote. It's on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. But it's not just for the president. Our democracy has this thing called the Electoral College that makes everything trickier. They're the ones who pick the president and vice president, and Congress counts what they decide. Often, political groups pick these people. Can't we find a better way to do this? It's something to think about, really.

Instead of us directly voting for the president, our votes help pick these electors in the Electoral College. They are the ones who make the big decision, not us. "What Is the Electoral College?" says when you vote, you're really backing electors who like a certain candidate. Pretty much anyone can be an elector, unless they have a powerful job. And once they pick, it's done. So complicated, right? "Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong" notes that over 60% of people want an easier way to vote. Most folks just want it simple.

The Electoral votes aren't given out evenly. States with lots of people, like California, get more votes than smaller states like Vermont. The House tries to make sure everyone is represented, but the Electoral College doesn't do that. It's not balanced. This is a problem because it doesn't seem fair at all.

People get mixed up about the Electoral College. Voters might not even know who they're supporting, and they can't control what the electors do. It's a problem when electors don't do what the voters want. "Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong" points this out. We choose electors, but they choose the President, so it's not a straight shot.

Some say getting rid of the Electoral College would hurt our country and our values. "In defense of the Electoral College..." argues that parties pick electors who should be for their candidate, but even if you get the most votes, you could still lose. You could vote, and it might not even matter. It stops extra elections if no one gets enough votes, but it still has issues.

Basically, the Electoral College isn't doing us any favors. It feels unfair and hard to understand. It might have some good points, but the bad ones are worse. It's not balanced and could mess things up. We should ditch it for a fairer America.

Alright, the Electoral College is quite the head-scratcher, isn't it? Considering it arose in a different era, one must wonder, do its original justifications still hold up today? Back then, the average citizen's knowledge of political affairs was limited, hindering informed voting. Now, however, we're bombarded with news and candidate information. Wouldn't a simple tally of everyone's votes offer a more equitable method for choosing the president? Ideally, the president should heed the collective voice of the people, not just a select group of influencers.

Even staunch supporters of the Electoral College acknowledge its flaws. While we believe we're directly electing the President, our votes actually go to electors, chosen at the state's discretion through varied methods. These electors pledge allegiance to a specific candidate and generally fulfill their promise. However, Bradford Plumer points out the limited control we have over electors and the potential for voter confusion leading to errors. Furthermore, electors occasionally defy their pledge and cast their vote elsewhere!

The Electoral College inflates the importance of "swing states"—those where either candidate has a shot at winning. Since the victor snags all of the state's electoral votes, voters in states with predictable leanings often feel disenfranchised and opt out of voting. This forces campaigns to hyper-focus on persuading voters in a handful of states. A popular vote system would compel candidates to engage with a broader range of concerns.

Opting for a president via popular vote presents itself as a vastly fairer approach. Even niche groups within reliably partisan states would gain a voice. Polls indicate that the majority of voters prefer direct presidential elections. Critics of popular vote even say that a popular vote can cause ties and make votes irrelevant, which in turn is a silly statement, and hurts voters and their turnout! Every vote holds significance, and equitable representation is paramount. A popular vote ensures a president who truly embodies the will of ALL Americans.

Post-World War II, having a car became super popular! Articles 2 and 3 talked about cities trying out days without cars. But, folks in articles 1 and 4 said they were already driving less. Less driving? Awesome! That means less pollution and less traffic jams!

Air pollution is a big problem. Rosenthal pointed out, "Cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases... and some U.S. places hit 50%! P5." So many cars mean yucky city smog. Remember the Beijing Olympics in '09? Cutting car use cleared the air fast! Really important for athletes and tourists, especially since everyone in Beijing was already wearing masks. It's tough, but less car use stops bad stuff. Duffer mentions Paris cutting pollution, even banning some license plates P19! Less car pollution means cleaner air and prettier views!

What's interesting is that many young people are skipping driver's licenses, even though they grew up with cars. Selsky says fewer cars mean space for parks, sports fields, shops, and walking spots P28! That's great for new businesses too! Instead of malls by highways, shopping is popping up near trains and buses, where walking is easy. Rosenthal also mentions his family using buses, carpools, and their own feet to drive less P40. Taking the bus or train? Smart! Carpooling helps a lot too. Thanks to carpools, walking, and buses, seeing the city and getting around is much easier.

Cities are changing to help people who don't use cars. Less car use leads to a lot of good things. Cleaner air and easier commutes are making cars less necessary. If we keep cutting back on cars, the future could be even better!

Alright, so here is the deal with the electoral college: Individuals are chosen to be electors, and they cast votes for the president; following that, the votes are tallied. However, does this represent an effective approach, or might it result in major issues? We need to abolish it! Since it's not a vote directly from the people, which might cause issues.

A case is made that the electoral college prevents difficult runoff elections, and that might simplify matters. Richard A. Posner observed that in extremely close races, people might prefer runoffs. Therefore, the argument goes, if the electoral college is retained, runoffs would happen less. But is that honestly ideal, and what problems may result? If electors determine the president, can we depend on them to prevent runoffs? Even though Posner believes it reduces the likelihood of interference, that minor benefit is insufficient to outweigh all of the serious drawbacks.

Visualize everything going wrong. We'd be in a real jam! The author notes we were fortunate that the 2000 election was only the worst in a century; the next time, it could be far worse. Historical precedent shows it has occurred before. Plumer believes the electoral college has harmed the U.S., and maintaining it may lead to further turmoil. The author reminds us that electors have previously defied their party, Plumer says. As a result, it serves no useful purpose and offers opportunities for manipulation. It needs to be eliminated because it has the ability to cripple our government.

Moreover, it doesn't truly represent a popular vote. As per the Office of the Federal Register, when you vote for a candidate, you are essentially voting for *their* electors. Thus, the leader of the country isn't directly chosen by the populace. It seems inappropriate for leaders to make decisions affecting people who didn't even vote for them. According to Plumer, it's unjust, antiquated, and simply foolish. These issues highlight the electoral college's negative impact on public confidence in the government. Plumer also points out that candidates neglect states they cannot win while only focusing on "swing" states. The pursuit of victory is more important than the country's best interests. Can we allow this to destroy us?

To summarize, the electoral college? Do away with it.

Esteemed Senator from Florida, The people who started our country made the electoral college so the president would be who most people wanted. But, because of this, someone could win without getting the most votes. This old system makes ties more likely. Lots of Americans want real democracy, and this system might stop that from happening.

The popular vote is the best way to know what the country wants. Most people, "over 60 percent," want to pick the president directly. People want their votes to matter. When the President sends troops to war, people want their opinions to be important, with their votes having meaning, instead of being ignored by the electoral college. Just 538 electors have way more power than tons of regular voters. We have rights like "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness," so freedom should include fair voting.

The electoral college has been around for a long time, which is why it's a problem. The U.S. needs to update its voting rules to stay current. As the world changes, the system needs to change too. We should be glad that "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century" because it could easily happen again.

Even worse, a tie could happen and make things even harder. Someone said that "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." Democracy shouldn't end in ties. The people's voice, not a tie, should decide who wins. Source 2 says, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe." A tie makes the popular vote useless, which is scary.

To sum it up, we should get rid of the electoral college because it hurts people. It makes our votes less important, it's old, and it could cause a tie. I hope we pick the president based on what the people want, not what people in D.C. think.

Alright, think about cars. Going to locations and encountering friends—that's their role. Suppose they just disappeared? Strange, right? However, here's the surprising part: surprisingly wonderful things might genuinely take place.

To begin, the planet benefits SUBSTANTIALLY! Cars create a lot of pollution, correct? See this: "Passenger cars? Account for 12% of Europe's total greenhouse gas emissions!" Also, "Up to 50% in heavily trafficked regions in the United States!" ("In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 5). Thus, without cars, we'd have cleaner air, and no horrible smog. Overall, significantly cleaner air!

Furthermore, we would all become healthier too. Of course, this comes from having cleaner air! In addition, fewer cars implies walking or cycling. "Vauban," according to "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 6, "is home to 5,500 inhabitants and has zero cars." Stores are nearby, so it's an "easy walk," and things aren't "miles away." Walk, bike, or take the bus. More movement, less sitting. Incredible!

Moreover, cars could already be going away! As stated in paragraph 29 of "The End of Car Culture," "Obama's emission cuts" were "aided by fewer car sales, fewer miles driven, and fewer licenses." Gas prices are high, texting causes accidents, and new drivers are confused. People are becoming less fond of cars. Great for our planet, yes? Driving may be disappearing soon.

In summary, if there were fewer cars, it would be a complete victory! The planet can breathe easier, and people are already getting rid of them. Why waste gas when you can walk, bike, and stay healthy? Less driving? Fantastic!

Ah, the Electoral College! This old system sure does get folks riled up. Should we ditch it, or leave it be? Some say it's just plain unfair, messing things up for voters and those running for president. But others? They swear it's the best way to pick a leader. With strong feelings on both sides, should we tweak it somehow?

Instead of just counting votes, the Electoral College tries to keep things fair in presidential races. Each state has its own group of electors chosen by political groups. So, when you vote, you're really backing the electors picked by the person you want as president.

So, why do some people want to get rid of the Electoral College? Well, things can get messy. One big worry is a tie. How could that happen? Electors might go rogue and vote their own way. Even though we think our votes choose electors, they might not do what we expect. This is a problem if electors ditch their party's pick. After Al Gore won the popular vote in 2000 but lost the election, lots of voters wanted direct elections, which made them really mad at the Electoral College.

But hey, there are also big fans who want to keep it around. Each party picks electors who will likely stick with their candidate, and they usually do. Parties like to pick people who are super loyal. Also, it shows how important it is to get support from different parts of the country. A president who only appeals to one area might not be able to lead the whole nation well. Just pleasing one group isn't enough. So, winning would be tough. Plus, the Electoral College can help when no one gets a clear majority.

In the end, thinking about what to do with the Electoral College – keep it or toss it – shows there are good reasons on both sides. It's up to us, the people, to decide.

Cars are super helpful for families when it comes to getting kids to school, activities, and more. Yet, an increasing number of people are now contemplating whether reducing our dependence on cars might have a real, positive impact on the environment.

It's common knowledge that cars are harmful to the planet. They contribute to air pollution and release gases that cause global warming, which is a significant concern. In Europe, cars are responsible for about 12% of these harmful emissions. However, in the US, where car usage is widespread, this figure can be as high as 50%! Heavy traffic only exacerbates these issues. For example, in France, diesel cars create problematic smog, especially during hot weather. Therefore, reducing car usage translates to cleaner air for everyone.

Furthermore, cutting back on driving can improve your well-being. Many people worldwide have experimented with car-free lifestyles, opting for alternative modes of transportation. Heidrun Walkter, a financially comfortable German mother, decided to sell her car. She now enjoys walking and the sounds of bikes and children. She stated, "I was constantly stressed when I had a car. Now, I'm more at peace." Additionally, reducing car use encourages increased physical activity. In Colombia, millions participated in a car-free day. They chose to walk, bike, or use public transportation to combat smog. It proved effective! People reported feeling more connected and less stressed. One man, riding his bike with his wife, remarked that it was beneficial for both the planet and their personal health.

Ultimately, reducing car usage is a straightforward approach to achieving cleaner air, less smog, and increased biking. From Germany and France to Colombia and the US, numerous countries are actively working to address the adverse effects associated with driving.

Listen up, Senator! You should really consider supporting the concept of choosing Presidents via popular vote; it's genuinely crucial, and here's why. Picture this: Electors could totally go rogue and disregard the will of the people. Furthermore, we might wind up with a deadlock, sending the decision to the House. Also, this whole winner-take-all approach? Utterly bonkers.

Seriously, can you even trust electors? They have been known to fail in the past. As Bradford Plumer pointed out, some folks nearly botched the '60 election by switching electors last minute. Believe it or not, they sometimes just cast their ballots how they please, even if it's against their own party – talk about a crazy turn of events!

An Electoral College tie scenario? Super frightening to consider. This would mean the House gets to play judge, jury, and executioner, with each state getting a single vote. Imagine: tiny Wyoming having the same influence as mega-California! Back in '76, a slight shift in votes in Ohio and Hawaii almost caused a tie. Now that's cutting it way too close.

The winner-take-all framework is just plain unfair to citizens. Candidates pour all their resources into swing states, ignoring the rest of the nation. Take 2000; many states got zero attention from candidates. A ton of city residents didn't even catch a single political ad. Are we seriously okay with voters in states like Ohio calling all the shots? It just doesn't sit right.

So, Senator, please understand this: advocate for a popular vote system when it comes to picking Presidents! Because electors could forsake voters, a tie sends things to the House, and winner-take-all is quite simply wrong and unfair to the people of America.
